result.question: Designing/training a voice/TTS model to be more comprehensible at high-speed listening: cover (1) acoustic/prosodic properties that survive time-compression (clear speech, vowel-space expansion, consonant emphasis, modulation spectrum, F0, pause/rhythm), (2) formant synthesis vs neural TTS at extreme speed and why blind power users prefer Eloquence/DECtalk/eSpeak-NG, (3) rate-aware/controllable TTS generation and whether native fast generation/fine-tuning beats post-hoc time-compression, (4) DSP enhancement for intelligibility at speed (clarity/formant enhancement, consonant boosting, per-phoneme variable-rate compression), (5) concrete engines/voices and config knobs (Kokoro, Piper, eSpeak-NG/Eloquence, RHVoice, Azure/Google, ElevenLabs), (6) how to measure comprehensibility at speed (STOI, STMI, modulation-spectrum, MUSHRA/A-B protocols). Constraint: local/on-device open-source pipeline (currently Kokoro), serving a natural default voice and an optional aggressively speed-optimized track, with per-effort-tier cost/gain/worth-it judgments. Prefer peer-reviewed + blind/screen-reader community knowledge.

angles.label: acoustic-science/clear-speech
angles.query: clear speech hyperarticulation intelligibility time-compressed speech vowel space expansion consonant enhancement modulation spectrum acoustic correlates
angles.rationale: Covers angle 1: the peer-reviewed phonetics/speech-science evidence for which acoustic-prosodic properties survive time compression and make sped-up speech intelligible.

angles.label: formant-synth-vs-neural/blind-power-users
angles.query: blind screen reader users prefer Eloquence DECtalk eSpeak formant synthesis over neural TTS fast speech intelligibility 300 words per minute
angles.rationale: Covers angle 2: practitioner/community knowledge plus any research on why formant synthesizers beat natural neural voices at extreme rates; intelligibility-vs-naturalness trade-off.

angles.label: rate-aware-TTS/fine-tune-vs-timestretch
angles.query: speaking rate controllable neural TTS fast speech generation fine-tuning vs time-scale modification intelligibility gain clear speech Lombard corpus
angles.rationale: Covers angle 3: the critical question of measurable comprehensibility gain from native fast generation/fine-tuning versus post-hoc time-compression.

angles.label: DSP-intelligibility-enhancement
angles.query: speech intelligibility enhancement DSP consonant boosting spectral shaping dynamic range compression non-uniform time compression preserve consonant transitions vowel steady-state
angles.rationale: Covers angle 4: engine-agnostic DSP methods including content-aware per-phoneme variable-rate compression and modulation-envelope enhancement.

angles.label: engines-config + measurement
angles.query: Kokoro Piper RHVoice eSpeak-NG TTS speed comparison speaking rate settings plus STOI STMI modulation spectrum intelligibility metric MUSHRA listening test protocol
angles.rationale: Covers angles 5 and 6: head-to-head local open-source engine/config comparison and objective+subjective methods to verify a 'significant gain' before committing to a fine-tune.

result.summary: Decomposition strategy: split the question along its two backbones — the underlying speech science (what makes fast speech intelligible) and the engineering decision ladder (pick/config a voice < DSP post-processing < fine-tune < train from scratch) — then map the six required sub-angles onto five non-redundant queries. Angles 1-4 each get a dedicated query targeting peer-reviewed acoustics, the blind/screen-reader formant-synthesis community, rate-aware/controllable TTS (with the pivotal fine-tune-vs-time-stretch gain comparison), and engine-agnostic DSP. The fifth query bundles the concrete local open-source engine/config comparison (angle 5) with the comprehensibility measurement methods (angle 6), since both feed the practical "is the gain worth it" verdict for a Kokoro-based local pipeline.

results.title: A spectro-temporal modulation index (STMI) for assessment of speech intelligibility (Elhilali, Chi & Shamma, Speech Communication 2003)
results.snippet: Canonical peer-reviewed source defining the STMI metric named in angle 6. STMI elaborates on the STI by incorporating joint (inseparable) spectro-temporal modulation dimensions, integrating filtered spectrograms across the acoustic-frequency axis to capture temporal energy fluctuation per modulation channel. This is the authoritative reference for an objective metric that is sensitive to exactly the spectro-temporal cues degraded by time-compression/speed-up, making it ideal for quantifying whether a fast-but-clear voice preserves intelligibility cues. Direct PDF from the Johns Hopkins LCAP lab.

results.title: Intelligibility of time-compressed synthetic speech: Compression method and speaking style (Speech Communication)
results.snippet: Directly tests the angle-6/angle-3 crux: it measures how synthetic-speech intelligibility at high speed depends on (a) the compression method (uniform vs non-uniform time-scale modification) and (b) the speaking style of the source voice (e.g., clear/hyperarticulated). This is the closest peer-reviewed evidence to the user's central question of whether a clear-speech source or smarter compression buys measurable intelligibility gains over naive time-compression, and it anchors which conditions to A/B in a listening test.

results.title: The State of Modern AI Text-To-Speech Systems for Screen Reader Users (2026 practitioner write-up)
results.snippet: Community/practitioner knowledge from the blind speed-listening world directly supporting angles 2 and 5. States sighted users prefer natural/human-like voices while blind power users prefer fast, clear, predictable, efficient voices, explaining the preference for robotic formant synths (Eloquence/DECtalk) understandable at 800-900 WPM vs ~200-250 WPM normal speech. Useful for justifying a separate aggressively speed-optimized track and for the head-to-head neural-vs-formant framing.

results.title: Crowdsourcing MUSHRA Tests in the Age of Generative Speech Technologies (Interspeech 2025)
results.snippet: Modern, TTS-specific MUSHRA protocol guidance for angle 6's subjective-evaluation requirement. Covers how to run reliable MUSHRA-style listening tests (hidden reference, anchors, 0-100 scale, ~10s stimuli, post-screening rule excluding raters who score the hidden reference <90 on >15% of items) adapted for generative speech and crowdsourcing. This is the practical recipe for deciding whether a fine-tune cleared a 'significant gain' bar before committing effort.

results.title: Rethinking MUSHRA: Addressing Modern Challenges in Text-to-Speech Evaluation (arXiv 2024)
results.snippet: Complements the Interspeech MUSHRA paper for angle 6 by critiquing classic MUSHRA when applied to high-quality modern TTS and proposing fixes (e.g., reference/anchor handling when synthetic samples rival natural speech). Relevant for designing an A/B/MUSHRA protocol that can still discriminate small intelligibility differences between a baseline Kokoro voice, a DSP-enhanced track, and a fine-tuned fast voice.

results.title: On the intelligibility of fast synthesized speech for individuals with early-onset blindness
results.snippet: Peer-reviewed quantification underpinning angles 2 and 5: measures intelligibility of synthesized speech at elevated rates for blind listeners, supporting the claim that trained power listeners comprehend speech far above normal rates and that rate is a dominant intelligibility lever. Useful as a citation for setting target syllable/word rates and for justifying the optional power-listener track plus the listening-test rate conditions to evaluate.

results.title: Non-linear time compression of clear and normal speech at high rates (Valentini-Botinhao et al.)
results.snippet: DIRECTLY bridges angle 1 to the user's pipeline. Compares time-compressing CLEAR speech vs NORMAL speech at high rates (up to 3x). Key findings: (a) compressed clear speech beats compressed normal speech up to a rate, but at 3x the clear-speech advantage VANISHES — quantifies the ceiling on how much native clear speech buys you over compressing a normal voice; (b) non-linear method: compress steady-state vowels MORE while slowing/preserving consonant-vowel formant transitions (the most confusable consonant pairs carry distinctive features in CV transitions); (c) compressing silence ~2x more than speech improves normal speech cheaply. This is the central acoustic-science evidence for content-aware per-phoneme variable-rate compression.

results.title: Acoustic correlates of vowel intelligibility in clear and conversational speech (Ferguson & Kewley-Port, JASA)
results.snippet: Peer-reviewed acoustic dissection of WHICH clear-speech features drive intelligibility: vowel duration, steady-state formant frequencies, and dynamic formant movement, measured for normal-hearing and hearing-impaired listeners. Establishes vowel-space expansion and formant-frequency shifts as measurable correlates of the clear-speech intelligibility benefit — the core acoustic targets a rate-aware TTS or DSP stage should preserve/enhance.

results.title: Acoustic correlates of intelligibility enhancements in clearly produced consonants (Maniwa, Jongman & Wade, Interspeech)
results.snippet: Open-access PDF focused specifically on CONSONANTS in clear speech — the segments most vulnerable to time-compression. Documents stronger/longer consonants, greater high-frequency energy concentration, and sharpened spectral cues. Directly supports the consonant-emphasis / sharper CV-transition and consonant-boosting recommendations (angles 1 and 4) for any TTS engine's output.

results.title: The Modulation Transfer Function for Speech Intelligibility (Elliott & Theunissen, PLoS Comput Biol)
results.snippet: Authoritative modulation-spectrum paper. Identifies the spectro-temporal modulation regions critical to intelligibility: temporal modulations peak near ~4 Hz and intelligibility collapses when modulations below ~4 cyc/kHz (the formant region) are removed. Provides the theoretical basis for modulation-spectrum analysis and STMI-style metrics (angle 6) and explains WHY time-compression hurts: it shifts the envelope spectrum away from the 4 Hz region the auditory system expects.

results.title: The possible role of brain rhythms in perceiving fast speech / theta-oscillator 'repackaging' (PMC)
results.snippet: Explains the neural-rhythm mechanism behind sped-up-speech breakdown and the 'repackaging' fix: time-compressed, unintelligible speech can be RESTORED to intelligibility by inserting regular silent gaps so the rhythm re-aligns with the ~theta (4-8 Hz) oscillator. Strong evidence that PAUSE/RHYTHM structure (not just spectral detail) is a survivable cue — concrete justification for compressing silence less, or re-inserting micro-pauses, in a fast track.

results.title: The effect of hyperarticulation on speech comprehension under adverse listening conditions (Psychological Research)
results.snippet: Tests hyperarticulation (the production mechanism behind clear speech: vowel-space expansion plus increased duration for fuller articulatory targets) under adverse listening — analogous to the degraded condition of high-speed listening. Useful for the 'natural default voice' use case: shows hyperarticulated/clear style helps comprehension when conditions are hard, but the benefit is bounded, reinforcing that duration-dependent gains erode as you compress time.

results.title: Intelligibility of time-compressed speech: uniform vs non-uniform time-compression algorithms (JASA 2014, Schlueter et al.)
results.snippet: Directly addresses the user's most load-bearing DSP question — does content-aware (non-uniform) compression beat uniform compression? Critically, this peer-reviewed JASA study found the OPPOSITE of the intuitive expectation in a speech-in-noise context: the non-uniform algorithm caused larger phoneme-duration/spectral/modulation-spectrum distortions and the UNIFORM algorithm showed a clear subjective and objective advantage. This is the essential caveat — non-uniform per-phoneme compression is not a guaranteed win and depends heavily on algorithm quality and listening condition. Quantifiable, controlled, and uses both objective and subjective measures.

results.title: Cochlea-scaled entropy, not consonants, vowels, or time, best predicts speech intelligibility (Stilp & Kluender, PNAS 2010)
results.snippet: Foundational science underpinning content-aware per-phoneme compression. Shows that intelligibility is best predicted by cochlea-scaled SPECTRAL CHANGE (high-entropy regions), not by the consonant/vowel category label per se — replacing high-entropy segments (which include CV/VC transitions) wrecks intelligibility while low-entropy (steady-state) segments are expendable. This justifies the 'compress steady-state, preserve transitions' strategy on a more principled basis than the crude consonant-vs-vowel heuristic, and gives an objective metric (cochlea-scaled entropy) for choosing what to compress.

results.title: Speech-in-noise intelligibility improvement based on spectral shaping and dynamic range compression (SSDRC, Zorila et al., Interspeech 2012)
results.snippet: The canonical engine-agnostic DSP recipe for the user's pipeline: a cascade of spectral shaping (boost F2/F3 relative to F1, adaptive high-pass to emphasize consonants/formants) followed by dynamic-range compression. SSDRC is a well-validated, low-latency, non-machine-learning post-processor that applies to ANY TTS output (Kokoro included) and produces large measured intelligibility gains in noise. Maps exactly onto the 'spectral shaping + DRC + consonant boosting' part of angle 4.

results.title: Real-Time Modulation Enhancement of Temporal Envelopes for Increasing Speech Intelligibility
results.snippet: Directly covers the 'modulation-envelope enhancement' sub-angle. Describes a real-time technique that deepens temporal amplitude modulations (independently in time and frequency domains) to raise intelligibility — relevant because clear speech and fast/compressed speech both hinge on modulation-spectrum shape (4-16 Hz envelope). Being real-time and DSP-based, it is compatible with a local Kokoro post-processing stage and applies to any engine's output.

results.title: Speech Intelligibility Prediction using Spectro-Temporal Modulation Analysis (STMI-style modulation index)
results.snippet: Bridges DSP enhancement with measurement (angle 6 overlap). Explains spectro-temporal modulation analysis / STMI as the backbone for predicting intelligibility under modulated-noise and reverberation degradations, and shows modulation-domain analysis substantially improves objective intelligibility prediction. Useful both for designing modulation-envelope enhancement and for objectively scoring whether a fast/compressed track preserved the diagnostic 4-16 Hz modulations before running listening tests.

results.title: Effect of Consonant Duration Modifications on Speech Perception in Noise
results.snippet: Supports the per-phoneme variable-rate strategy with direct evidence: lengthening/preserving consonant duration (while vowels are compressed) reduces vowel-on-consonant masking and improves perception, weakening the need for the 'repeat to hear the consonant' effect. Provides the phonetic-cue rationale for why a content-aware compressor should protect consonants and their transitions while compressing high-energy steady-state vowels — directly actionable for an aggressively speed-optimized power-listener track.

results.title: Recognition of time-compressed speech does not predict recognition of natural fast-rate speech by older listeners (PMC)
results.snippet: Direct, quantified answer to the core question and a crucial counterintuitive result: artificially time-compressed (TSM) speech is recognized CONSISTENTLY BETTER than naturally produced fast speech at matched rates (gap widens in noise, p=0.005 for older listeners). Reason: natural fast speech suffers segmental deletions, stop/affricate distortions, schwa deletion and articulation undershoot, whereas TSM uniformly preserves spectral/temporal structure. Implication for the pipeline: simply generating 'fast' speech does NOT beat time-compressing a normal voice unless the model specifically targets clear articulation. This sets the bar a native-fast fine-tune must clear.

results.title: Word perception in fast speech: artificially time-compressed vs. naturally produced fast speech (Speech Communication, Janse)
results.snippet: Canonical peer-reviewed study establishing that even when naturally produced fast speech is fully intelligible, its less-careful articulation plus changed timing pattern SLOWS DOWN word processing relative to linear time-compression. Linearly time-compressed speech has both a temporal and a segmental processing advantage, and listeners prefer it. Core evidence that post-hoc TSM is a strong baseline; a native-fast neural TTS must add clear-speech articulation to win.

results.title: Enhancing Speech Intelligibility in Text-To-Speech Synthesis using Speaking Style Conversion / Lombard-SSDRC (arXiv)
results.snippet: Quantifies the measurable gain from fine-tuning/conditioning a neural TTS toward a clear/Lombard speaking style rather than just speeding it up. Lombard-SSDRC TTS yields 110-130% relative intelligibility improvement (SIIB) in speech-shaped noise and 47-140% in competing-speaker noise vs. SOTA TTS; subjective keyword-correct gains up to 455%. Shows WHERE the real comprehensibility payoff of training lives (clear/Lombard articulation), distinct from raw speaking-rate increase. Directly informs the 'is fine-tuning worth it' decision.

results.title: FastSpeech: TTS model with length-regulator speaking-rate control (Microsoft Research)
results.snippet: Foundational reference for NATIVE rate-controllable neural TTS: FastSpeech's length regulator scales the phoneme-duration predictor to vary speed 0.5x-1.5x without quality loss, and exposes per-phoneme duration so vowels vs. consonants can be compressed differently (content-aware variable-rate). This is the mechanism by which a neural model can natively generate fast speech with controllable rhythm rather than post-hoc TSM. Same non-attentive duration-modeling family underlies Kokoro/Piper, so the knobs are locally available.

results.title: Lombard Speech Synthesis for Any Voice with Controllable Style Embeddings (arXiv)
results.snippet: State-of-the-art on conditioning/fine-tuning neural TTS to produce clear/Lombard speech with controllable style embeddings while preserving naturalness and speaker identity, with fine-grained prosody control. Most directly applicable recipe for the user's 'fine-tune an open model' effort tier: shows you can add a clear-speech style axis to an existing voice rather than training from scratch, and reports intelligibility-in-noise gains. Speaks to whether the fine-tune clears a significant-gain bar.

results.title: Covertly improving intelligibility with data-driven adaptations of speech timing (arXiv)
results.snippet: Nuanced cautionary evidence on rate/timing manipulation: listeners JUDGE targeted speech-rate restructuring as less intelligible than a general slowdown, yet the general slowdown actually raises word error rate. Highlights that subjective preference and objective intelligibility can diverge for timing/rate edits, which matters for designing the A/B/MUSHRA evaluation and for choosing per-phoneme variable-rate compression strategy over uniform changes. Supports the 'measure before committing' requirement.

results.title: The State of Modern AI Text To Speech Systems for Screen Reader Users (Samuel Proulx / eSpeak-NG discussion, Jan 2026)
results.snippet: The single most directly on-point source for angle 2. The author (a blind power user) and the eSpeak-NG community lay out exactly WHY formant synths beat neural TTS at speed. Full article (stuff.interfree.ca/2026/01/05/ai-tts-for-screenreaders.html) gives four concrete failure modes of neural/AI TTS for fast screen-reader use: (1) ACCURACY — neural models skip words, misread numbers, chop off short utterances, ignore prosody hints (unacceptable when scanning); (2) LATENCY — models like Supertonic/Kitten TTS can't start until they have a whole text chunk, so they can't instantly discard-and-restart on rapid navigation the way formant synths can; (3) CONTROL — formant synths expose pitch, speed, volume, breathiness, roughness, headsize as live knobs, neural models trained on fixed speakers can't; (4) DEPENDENCY BLOAT — neural models pull 30-103+ Python packages, slowing NVDA startup. Confirms formant voices are 'tiny, very flexible, intelligible at high speeds' and that Eloquence/eSpeak run at low latency. Directly relevant to the local/on-device Kokoro constraint.

results.title: The State of Modern AI Text-To-Speech Systems for Screen Reader Users (full article, Samuel Proulx)
results.snippet: The full text behind the eSpeak-NG discussion. States blind users prefer voices that are 'fast, clear, predictable, and efficient' over natural-sounding ones, understood 'upwards of 800 to 900 words per minute' vs 200-250 WPM for normal human speech. Quantifies the intelligibility-vs-naturalness trade-off as a practitioner sees it and explains the consistency/crispness/predictability advantages that make older formant synths the power-user choice. Useful for distinguishing the 'aggressively speed-optimized track' from the natural default voice in the user's two-track pipeline.

results.title: Ultra-fast speech comprehension in blind subjects engages primary visual cortex, fusiform gyrus, and pulvinar — an fMRI study (BMC Neuroscience, 2013)
results.snippet: Peer-reviewed neuroscience providing the hard numbers for the original question's 18-22 syl/s claim. Blind listeners can comprehend speech up to ~22 syllables/second vs ~8 syl/s ceiling for normal-sighted listeners. Tested moderately-fast (8 syl/s) vs ultra-fast (16 syl/s) rates. Shows the comprehension ceiling is a trainable neural-plasticity phenomenon (cross-modal recruitment of V1/fusiform/pulvinar), which explains why only 'trained power listeners' can use the aggressive track — grounding the user's two-audience design decision in evidence.

results.title: Training of ultra-fast speech comprehension induces functional reorganization of the central-visual system in late-blind humans (Frontiers in Human Neuroscience, 2013)
results.snippet: Companion training study: late-blind participants acquired ultra-fast (18 syl/s) speech-perception skills, and the skill induced de novo visual-cortex activation. Demonstrates the high-speed comprehension is a learned/trainable ability rather than innate — directly supports the idea that an 'optional aggressively speed-optimized track for trained power listeners' is viable and that comprehensibility at extreme rates improves with exposure. Reinforces measuring gains against a trained-listener population.

results.title: The 'voice' has it: screen reader adoption and switching behavior among vision-impaired persons (PMC)
results.snippet: Empirical user study where the Eloquence TTS voice was 'the single most mentioned element of preference' among vision-impaired screen-reader users, and a key statement that blind users find FORMANT synths easier to comprehend when sped up than CONCATENATED ones — directly supporting the synthesis-method intelligibility argument. eSpeak on NVDA was called mechanical/unattractive but intelligibility was rarely questioned. Provides survey-grade evidence for the formant-preference claim beyond anecdote.

results.title: Extraordinary Ability of Blind People to Hear Ultrafast Speech (R. Douglas Fields, Scientific American contributor)
results.snippet: Accessible science-journalism summary of the ultra-fast-speech research, useful for framing the phenomenon (blind listeners parsing speech far faster than sighted listeners) for a general/practitioner audience. Good background bridge between the peer-reviewed fMRI papers and the engineering recommendations; lower relevance than primary sources but helpful context on why intelligibility at extreme rates matters and is achievable.

claims.claim: Generating synthetic speech at a normal speaking rate and then applying linear time-compression to the waveform produced the most intelligible speech at ALL tested rates for English voices — i.e., post-hoc linear compression of a normal voice beat duration-model-based native fast generation. This directly bears on whether native fast generation buys measurable comprehensibility over simple time-compression: for English it did NOT.
claims.quote: Word recognition results for the English voices show that generating speech at a normal speaking rate and then applying linear compression resulted in the most intelligible speech at all tested rates, with similar results found when evaluating the intelligibility of the natural speech corpus.

claims.claim: Training a TTS system on fast speech data does not reliably yield more intelligible fast voices; linear compression of normal-rate speech more reliably gives higher intelligibility, especially at higher compression rates. This is a direct argument against investing in fast-speech fine-tuning/training when the cheaper post-hoc compression path is available.
claims.quote: Using fast speech data does not necessarily create more intelligible voices and linear compression can more reliably provide higher intelligibility, particularly at higher rates.

claims.claim: Three compression/generation approaches were compared head-to-head: scaling the variance of the state duration model, interpolating the duration models of the fast and normal voices, and applying linear compression to the generated waveform — providing a concrete native-generation-vs-post-hoc-DSP comparison framework.
claims.quote: Three compression methods were compared: scaling the variance of the state duration model, interpolating the duration models of the fast and the normal voices, and applying a linear compression method to the generated speech waveform.

claims.claim: The advantage of native/duration-model methods is rate-dependent: for German voices, interpolating fast and normal duration models was better at MODERATE speaking rates, but linear waveform compression was again more successful at VERY HIGH rates (the regime relevant to power-listener fast tracks).
claims.quote: For the German voices, interpolation was found to be better at moderate speaking rates but the linear method was again more successful at very high rates, particularly when applied to the fast data.

claims.claim: Comprehensibility-at-speed was measured via word-recognition intelligibility experiments on both natural and synthetic speech across a range of compression rates, with both sighted and blind listeners — a usable measurement protocol for deciding whether a method clears a significant-gain bar.
claims.quote: The paper presents a series of intelligibility experiments performed on natural and synthetic speech time-compressed at a range of rates and analyzes the effect of speech corpus and compression method on the intelligibility scores of sighted and blind individuals.

claims.claim: When time-compressed at high rates, NORMAL speech remains more intelligible than CLEAR (hyperarticulated) speech — even after equalizing total speech+silence duration — challenging the assumption that clear-speech corpora are the best training target for fast TTS.
claims.quote: We found that even when the overall duration of speech and silence is kept the same across styles, compressed normal speech is still more intelligible than compressed clear speech.

claims.claim: Linearly time-compressed NORMAL speech is more intelligible than natural fast (originally fast-produced) speech, indicating post-hoc uniform time-compression of a normal voice can beat natively-produced fast speech at moderate rates.
claims.quote: We found, in line with the literature, that unprocessed fast speech was less intelligible than linearly compressed normal speech.

claims.claim: Clear speech's intelligibility advantage over fast speech disappears at extreme compression (about 3x normal rate), so clear-speech benefits do not survive the highest speeds.
claims.quote: Fast speech was also less intelligible than compressed clear speech but at the highest rate (three times faster than normal) the advantage of clear over fast speech was lost.

claims.claim: A simple non-uniform DSP strategy — compressing silence/pauses about twice as much as speech — further improves intelligibility of compressed normal speech at negligible computational cost, supporting content-aware variable-rate compression for a local pipeline.
claims.quote: Compressing silence twice as much as speech improved results further for normal speech with very little additional computational costs.

claims.claim: Standard MUSHRA suffers from reference-matching bias: because raters anchor on the human reference, modern TTS systems that exceed human-speech quality are unduly penalised, biasing scores downward.
claims.quote: its reliance on matching human reference speech unduly penalises the scores of modern TTS systems that can exceed human speech quality

claims.claim: The paper identifies two primary shortcomings of MUSHRA for TTS evaluation: reference-matching bias and judgement ambiguity from a lack of fine-grained rating guidelines.
claims.quote: reference-matching bias, where raters are unduly influenced by the human reference ... judgement ambiguity, arising from a lack of clear fine-grained guidelines

claims.claim: The authors propose two refined MUSHRA variants: one that enables fairer ratings for synthesized samples surpassing human-reference quality, and one that reduces rater ambiguity, evidenced by lower cross-rater variance.
claims.quote: We propose two refined variants of the MUSHRA test. The first variant enables fairer ratings for synthesized samples that surpass human reference quality. The second variant reduces ambiguity, as indicated by the relatively lower variance across raters.

claims.claim: MOS tests fail to differentiate between similar (high-quality, closely-matched) models and CMOS pairwise comparisons are time-intensive, motivating improved MUSHRA-style protocols for distinguishing near-equivalent TTS systems.
claims.quote: MOS tests fail to differentiate between similar models, and CMOS's pairwise comparisons are time-intensive

claims.claim: The study's empirical scale was 492 human listeners across Hindi and Tamil, producing a released dataset (MANGO) of 246,000 human ratings, giving a concrete benchmark for the listener/sample counts needed in TTS listening tests.
claims.quote: 492 human listeners across Hindi and Tamil ... MANGO, a massive dataset of 246,000 human ratings, the first-of-its-kind collection for Indian languages

claims.claim: Speaking rate has a significant effect on the intelligibility of fast synthesized speech for early-onset blind listeners, while synthesizer type shows only a trend toward significance (not a strong effect) in this pilot study.
claims.quote: The study found a significant effect of speaking rate on intelligibility of synthesized speech, and a trend towards significance for synthesizer type.

claims.claim: The study compared four TTS systems spanning two major synthesis approaches — formant-based synthesis and concatenative unit-selection synthesis — using an open-response recall task with early-onset blind participants.
claims.quote: The researchers collected data on four synthesis systems representing two major approaches to text-to-speech synthesis: formant-based synthesis and concatenative unit selection synthesis using an open-response recall task.

claims.claim: Participant-related factors, specifically age and familiarity with a given synthesizer and voice, measurably affect the intelligibility of fast synthesized speech — implying that power-listener familiarity, not just acoustic properties, drives comprehension at speed.
claims.quote: Post-hoc analyses found that participant-related factors, including age and familiarity with a synthesizer and voice, also affect intelligibility of fast synthesized speech.

claims.claim: Formant synthesis has intelligibility advantages at high syllable rates due to its simple rule-based structure, and both blind and sighted listeners can comprehend synthesized speech up to approximately 18 syllables per second.
claims.quote: research findings indicate that formant synthesis, due to its simple rule-based structure seems to have even some advantages regarding intelligibility at high syllable rates. Both blind and sighted subject groups were able to enhance speech comprehension up to approximately 18 syllables per second.

claims.claim: Visually impaired (blind) listeners can understand synthesized speech at higher speaking rates than sighted listeners, motivating an aggressively speed-optimized track for trained power listeners.
claims.quote: The research demonstrated that visually impaired people are able to listen and understand synthesized speech at higher speech rates than sighted people.

claims.claim: Crowdsourced MUSHRA tests, when designed with appropriate listener screening and procedural adjustments, can replicate expert-lab codec rankings with high test-retest reliability, with Prolific producing per-condition mean scores correlating with internal expert scores at Pearson r = 0.95 (Spearman 0.91) and MTurk at Pearson r = 0.89-0.90 (Spearman 0.89).
claims.quote: both Prolific and MTurk listeners produce the same overall ranking of codecs as Internal listeners (i.e., “ground truth”). However, Prolific’s scores more closely trace the Internal curve... The high correlation values for both Prolific runs confirm that the per-file mean scores closely track the internal scores. By contrast, both MTurk tests maintain somewhat lower correlations

claims.claim: To obtain stable crowdsourced MUSHRA results, approximately 15 responses per test item are needed on Prolific and around 25 responses per item on MTurk.
claims.quote: For Prolific, we observed stable results with approximately 15 responses for each test item, while MTurk required around 25 responses per test item.

claims.claim: For crowdsourced non-expert MUSHRA listeners, a maximum of 6 conditions per test should not be exceeded (versus the ITU BS.1534-3 maximum of 12 conditions including reference and anchors recommended for expert listeners), and participants should complete at most 3 rating blocks to prevent fatigue.
claims.quote: For expert-listener tests, a maximum of 12 conditions is recommended to be evaluated in parallel, including the reference and all anchors [9]... In our experience, a maximum of 6 conditions should not be exceeded in a crowdsourced test.

claims.claim: Listeners should be disqualified if they fail basic quality checks on more than 20% of MUSHRA questions in a block, where a failure is the anchor being rated higher than the hidden reference or all non-anchor ratings being identical (zero variance); pre-screening required >97% task success rate and >=100 completed tasks plus a digits-in-noise hearing test.
claims.quote: Listeners are disqualified entirely if they fail basic quality checks on more than 20% of the MUSHRA questions in a test block. A failure occurs if either of the following conditions is met for a given question: 1. The anchor is rated higher than the hidden reference... 2. All ratings for the non-anchor systems are identical

claims.claim: Traditional intrusive objective metrics (PESQ, POLQA, ViSQOL) systematically undervalue DNN/generative-based codecs versus DSP codecs, while the newer reference-based metric SCOREQ correlates most strongly and consistently with subjective scores across architectures (Pearson ~0.80 for both DNN and DSP codecs), making subjective MUSHRA testing necessary for generative speech models.
claims.quote: POLQA, PESQ, and ViSQOL correlated better with subjective scores for DSP-based codecs and systematically undervalued DNN-based codecs... By contrast, SCOREQ shows the strongest overall correlation with Prolific scores. The predictions remain consistently reliable across both DNN- and DSP-based codecs.

claims.claim: Clear speech increases vowel duration substantially over conversational speech (roughly 290 ms vs 204 ms, a ~1.5x increase), and clear speech significantly altered all acoustic metrics analyzed (duration, steady-state formants, and dynamic formant movement), though not for every vowel.
claims.quote: Talkers increased their vowel duration when speaking clearly, producing average vowel durations of 290 ms in clear speech and 204 ms in conversational speech.

claims.claim: Clear speech expands the vowel/formant space: front vowels show significantly higher F2 and back vowels significantly lower F2 in clear speech, expanding the F2 range (vowel-space spread) relative to conversational speech.
claims.quote: All five front vowels...showed significantly higher F2 values in clear speech, while the back vowels...had significantly lower F2 values.

claims.claim: The acoustic correlate most predictive of vowel intelligibility is greater LINEAR dynamic formant movement across the vowel nucleus (vowel-inherent spectral change), whereas non-linear within-nucleus movement is unrelated to intelligibility; this identifies dynamic spectral transitions, not just static spectral targets, as the intelligibility-bearing cue.
claims.quote: Greater linear formant movement between the endpoints of the vowel nucleus (VL) was associated with higher vowel intelligibility, while non-linear movement within the nucleus (TLadj) was unrelated to vowel intelligibility.

claims.claim: Clear speech yields a measurable, statistically significant intelligibility-in-noise benefit for vowels (odds ratio ~1.34 for a correct response, p < 0.0001 versus conversational speech).
claims.quote: The log-odds of a correct response increase by β = 0.293 (odds ratio 1.34, p < 0.0001) in clear versus conversational speech.

claims.claim: Which acoustic cues drive intelligibility depends on the listener: the contribution of duration and steady-state formant information to vowel intelligibility differed between young normal-hearing and elderly hearing-impaired listeners, implying intelligibility optimizations must be tuned to the target audience.
claims.quote: Mixed-effects analyses revealed that the contribution of duration and steady-state formant information to vowel intelligibility differed for the two listener groups.

claims.claim: Blind screen-reader power users prefer voices that are fast, clear, predictable, and efficient, listening at 800-900 words per minute versus the 200-250 wpm of an average speaker, accepting a robotic sound as a trade-off for accuracy.
claims.quote: blind users tend to prefer voices that are fast, clear, predictable, and efficient...understood at high rates of speed, often upwards of 800 to 900 words per minute. ... The speaking rate of an average person hovers around 200 to 250 words per minute, for comparison.

claims.claim: Modern neural TTS systems are deemed unsuitable for screen readers because their human-natural design comes at the expense of accuracy, with engines like Kitten TTS and Supertonic tending to skip words, misread numbers, chop off short utterances, and ignore prosody hints.
claims.quote: these modern systems are developed to sound human, natural, and conversational. Unfortunately this seems to come at the expense of accuracy. ... had a tendency to skip words, read numbers incorrectly, chop off short utterances, and ignore prosody hints.

claims.claim: Neural TTS introduces a latency/responsiveness problem fatal for screen-reader use: it cannot begin generating speech until it has an entire chunk of text, whereas screen-reader users frequently interrupt and need the engine to instantly discard and restart speech.
claims.quote: Unlike older text to speech systems, Supertonic and Kitten TTS cannot begin generating speech until they have an entire chunk of text. ... Users of screen readers quickly jump through text and frequently interrupt the screen reader, and thus require the text to speech system to be able to quickly discard and restart speech.

claims.claim: Legacy/formant synthesizers allow easy real-time control of pitch, speed, volume, breathiness, roughness, and head size, while neural models trained on fixed speaker data cannot offer this customization.
claims.quote: Older text to speech systems make changing the pitch, speed, volume, breathiness, roughness, headsize, and other parameters of the voice easy. ... AI text to speech models, being trained on data from a particular set of speakers, cannot offer this customization.

claims.claim: Eloquence, the synthesizer overwhelmingly preferred by Western English blind users, was last updated in 2003 and is a 32-bit binary that cannot run in modern software without an emulation layer, illustrating why no modern neural voice has displaced legacy formant synthesis.
claims.quote: the voice that is preferred by the majority of western English blind users, called Eloquence, was last updated in 2003 ... a 32-bit voice last compiled in 2003, [that] cannot run in modern software without some sort of emulation or bridge.

claims.claim: Intelligibility of fast time-compressed speech can be substantially restored ('rescued') by inserting silent gaps that 'repackage' the speech into syllable-rate packets within the theta band (3-8 Hz), and this effect is strongest at the fastest speech rates. This implies a DSP/timing-aware compression strategy (preserving syllable-rate packaging rather than uniform compression) can improve comprehension at speed.
claims.quote: the intelligibility of time-compressed speech was rescued by repackaging, especially at the faster speech rates.

claims.claim: The brain decodes speech using a time-varying windowing structure synchronized to the input across multiple timescales (TEMPO model), so intelligibility depends on the input's modulation matching neural oscillation rates rather than purely on raw speed.
claims.quote: speech decoding is performed within a time-varying window structure, synchronized with the input on multiple time scales.

claims.claim: The optimal repackaging/recovery rate falls within the theta frequency band of 3-8 Hz, with young adults showing best rescue around 5 packets per second and older adults around 4 packets per second, indicating a hard upper limit on the syllable-packet rate the brain can track.
claims.quote: Young adults showed successful repackaging rescue at 5 packets per second (within typical theta range), while older adults showed rescue at 4 packets per second

claims.claim: Intelligibility declines sharply as speech rate increases, with a compression factor of about 3 (tested across roughly 6-21 syllables per second) producing a steep drop in accuracy, setting a practical ceiling for how fast speech can be compressed before comprehension collapses.
claims.quote: Speech compression factor: Factor of 3 caused sharp intelligibility decline

claims.claim: There is a close correspondence between the timescales of phonemic, syllabic, and phrasal linguistic units and the periods of neural oscillations, with speech energy naturally fluctuating in the 3-20 Hz range tied to syllabic structure.
claims.quote: remarkable correspondence between the time scales of phonemic, syllabic, and phrasal linguistic units

claims.claim: Spectral shaping followed by dynamic range compression (SSDRC) — a two-stage cascade that enhances speech first in frequency then in time, under an equal-global-power constraint — outperforms prior state-of-the-art methods on the Speech Intelligibility Index (SII).
claims.quote: Two systems are combined in a cascade form to enhance the quality of the signal first in frequency (spectral shaping) and then in time (dynamic range compression). Experiments with speech shaped (SSN) and competed speaker (CS) types of noise at various low SNR values, show that the suggested approach outperforms state-of-the art methods in terms of the Speech Intelligibility Index (SII).

claims.claim: SSDRC yields an equivalent SNR-gain improvement of 4 dB under speech-shaped noise and 8 dB under a competing-speaker masker, meaning the enhanced speech is as intelligible as unprocessed speech at a 4-8 dB higher SNR.
claims.quote: In terms of SNR gain there is an improvement of 4 dB (SSN) and 8 dB (CS).

claims.claim: The intelligibility enhancement is achieved without raising overall signal energy — global signal power is held equal before and after processing — so gains come from spectral/temporal redistribution rather than simple amplification.
claims.quote: The signal is enhanced before presented in a noisy environment, under the constraint of equal global signal power before and after modifications.

claims.claim: The SSDRC intelligibility gains were confirmed by a large formal human listening test, not only by the objective SII metric.
claims.quote: A large formal listening test confirm the efficiency of the suggested system in enhancing speech intelligibility in noise.

claims.claim: Human speech intelligibility shows band-pass tuning in the temporal modulation domain (1-7 Hz most important) and low-pass tuning in the spectral modulation domain, defining the modulation regions that must be preserved for comprehensible speech.
claims.quote: The human speech intelligibility transfer function appears therefore to show a band-pass tuning in the temporal domain and a low-pass tuning in the spectral domain.

claims.claim: Intermediate temporal modulations between 1 and 7 Hz are the most critical band for speech intelligibility; since these correspond to syllable rate, time-compressing speech shifts energy out of this band and degrades comprehensibility.
claims.quote: intermediate temporal modulations between 1 and 7 Hz are critical for speech intelligibility

claims.claim: Comprehension is significantly impaired when temporal modulations below 12 Hz or spectral modulations below 4 cycles/kHz are removed, identifying concrete cutoff thresholds for modulation-spectrum shaping aimed at preserving intelligibility.
claims.quote: Comprehension was significantly impaired when temporal modulations <12 Hz or spectral modulations <4 cycles/kHz were removed.

claims.claim: Restricting speech to a 'core' low-pass modulation region of 3.75 cycles/kHz spectral and 7.75 Hz temporal still yields 75% word recognition, quantifying the minimal modulation content needed for intelligible speech.
claims.quote: Comprehension of core modulations was 75% word recognition

claims.claim: The speech signal carries substantial modulation redundancy: notch-filtering out modulations outside the core region leaves intelligibility excellent even at 2 dB SNR, implying much of the modulation spectrum can be removed without harming comprehension.
claims.quote: the intelligibility of most notch-filtered stimuli remained excellent

claims.claim: Clear-speech intelligibility enhancement for fricatives, in normal-hearing listeners, is driven primarily by a shift of acoustic energy toward higher-frequency regions and greater source strength — meaning consonant clarity at high frequencies (not low) is what carries the intelligibility gain. This is directly relevant to consonant-emphasis / high-frequency clarity strategies for fast-speech intelligibility.
claims.quote: Correlation analyses comparing acoustic and perceptual data indicated that a shift of energy concentration toward higher frequency regions and greater source strength contributed to the clear speech effect for normal-hearing listeners, while listeners with simulated loss seemed to benefit mostly from cues involving lower frequency regions.

claims.claim: Spectral measures (DFT and LPC peak location, spectral moment M1) were the strongest predictors of intelligibility improvement; positive correlations (p<0.05) between intelligibility advantage and these measures, and negative correlations for M3, indicate energy is shifted to higher frequency in clearer fricatives.
claims.quote: Overall, spectral measures appeared to be the important predictors for improved intelligibility. Significant positive correlations (p<0.05) were obtained between intelligibility advantages and acoustic modifications in DFT and LPC peak location and spectral moment 1 (M1), whereas significant negative correlations were found for M3. These results suggest that a shift of spectral energy to higher frequency regions in clear fricatives is most closely related to the overall intelligibility enhancement.

claims.claim: Clear speech significantly lowers the SNR threshold needed to identify fricative contrasts (more intelligible), but the benefit is sibilant-dominated: the effect was larger for sibilant pairs than non-sibilant pairs, with sibilants showing lower thresholds overall.
claims.quote: The Style × Sibilance × Distinction type ANOVA revealed a main effect of Sibilance (p<.001) with lower thresholds for sibilants than for non-sibilants... while both sibilants and non-sibilants were more intelligible in clear speech, the effect was larger for sibilant pairs.

claims.claim: Among the 14 acoustic measures examined per speaker, the clear-speech difference set included fricative duration, RMS amplitude, relative amplitude, harmonic-to-noise ratio, spectral peak location, and spectral moments — but spectral (frequency-location) cues, not duration or amplitude, emerged as the dominant intelligibility predictors for normal-hearing listeners.
claims.quote: a single clear-minus-conversational difference value, averaged over all fricatives and productions, was calculated for each of the 14 acoustic measures reported in [8] at fricative midpoint: spectral peak location, the first four spectral moments, F2 onset transitions, spectral slopes below and above typical peak locations, pitch of adjacent vowels, overall RMS amplitude, relative amplitude (frication amplitude relative to the vowel in specific frequency bands), harmonic-to-noise ratio, energy below 500 Hz, and fricative duration.

claims.claim: The clear-speech intelligibility benefit is population-dependent and can reverse: for simulated hearing-impaired listeners the acoustic correlates ran in the opposite direction (low-frequency cues helped), and for the non-sibilant /f/-/θ/ pair clear speech even produced a non-significant anti-clear-speech effect (higher thresholds). This bounds how generalizable a single clarity-enhancement strategy is across listener groups.
claims.quote: In fact, for /f/-/θ/, clear speech resulted in higher (n.s.) thresholds compared to conversational speech... For hearing-impaired listeners, however, effective alterations may be generally in the opposite direction and are probably more fricative-dependent.

claims.claim: Non-uniform (content-aware) time-compression caused greater deviations from the target compression and larger changes to phoneme duration, spectra, and modulation spectra than uniform compression, making uniform compression the recommended/superior approach for speech-in-noise testing.
claims.quote: The non-uniform algorithm showed "greater deviations from the targeted time compression, as well as greater changes of the phoneme duration, spectra, and modulation spectra" ... The uniform algorithm demonstrated superior performance for speech-in-noise testing applications compared to the non-uniform approach

claims.claim: Speech intelligibility decreases as speech rate increases; speech had to be compressed to more than 30% of its original length to reach 50% intelligibility at positive SNRs, quantifying how severely intelligibility degrades under extreme time compression.
claims.quote: Results showed "decreasing intelligibility with increasing speech rate" and that "speech had to be compressed to more than 30% of its original length to reach 50% intelligibility at positive SNRs."

claims.claim: The study was an empirical, controlled listening experiment using the German Oldenburg Sentence Test with 48 normal-hearing listeners, comparing uniform and non-uniform time-compression algorithms.
claims.quote: They compared uniform and non-uniform compression algorithms using the German Oldenburg Sentence Test ... 48 normal-hearing listeners participated

claims.claim: Recognition of time-compressed (artificially sped-up) speech systematically over-estimates recognition of naturally-produced fast speech, so time-compression is not an acoustically equivalent stand-in for native fast speech.
claims.quote: Recognition of time-compressed sentences over-estimated recognition of natural fast sentences for both groups, especially for older listeners.

claims.claim: Listeners recognize naturally fast speech considerably less accurately than uniformly time-compressed speech at a comparable rate, with the gap largest for low-context sentences.
claims.quote: Recognition scores are considerably lower for naturally fast speech than for either 40TC speech or normal-rate speech, particularly for the sentences with reduced contextual cues.

claims.claim: Intelligibility ranks normal-rate best, uniform time-compression intermediate, and natural fast speech worst, holding across quiet and noise conditions.
claims.quote: Performance was best for the normal rate speech, significantly poorer for the 40TC rate, and poorest with natural fast speech.

claims.claim: Natural fast speech and time-compressed speech differ acoustically: natural fast speech shortens phonemes but also deletes and distorts consonants (especially stops/affricates), whereas time-compression uniformly preserves fine structure and spectra in a reduced time scale.
claims.quote: Preliminary acoustic analyses of the natural fast recordings show not only shorter overall phoneme durations, but also deletions and distortions, especially of stops and affricates.

claims.claim: Hyperarticulated (clear) speech improved comprehension over non-hyperarticulated speech across all listener groups (native L1, early L2, late L2 learners) in BOTH quiet and noisy conditions, confirming clear-speech benefits hold under adverse listening.
claims.quote: all three groups found hyperarticulated speech samples easier to understand than non-hyperarticulated speech at all listening conditions.

claims.claim: The clear-speech intelligibility benefit was driven primarily by expanded vowel space; the hyperarticulated/foreign-directed condition had a significantly larger vowel space than the native-directed condition.
claims.quote: A comparison between the foreign-sounding conditions and the native-sounding conditions revealed that the vowel space was significantly larger for the foreign-sounding conditions than the native-sounding conditions.

claims.claim: The effect of speech style (hyperarticulated vs not) on comprehension was very large, with a partial eta-squared of 0.820, indicating clear-speech style accounts for the large majority of variance in comprehension ratings.
claims.quote: Main effect of interlocutor type: F(1, 45) = 205.002, p < 0.05, η²ₚ = 0.820

claims.claim: The adverse listening conditions were white-noise masking at SNRs of +16, +12, and +8 dB (plus quiet); comprehension was rated on a 1-6 ease-of-understanding scale, and added noise had a strong significant effect (η²ₚ = 0.725).
claims.quote: quiet or SNR levels of + 16 dB, + 12 dB or + 8 dB ... Noise effect: F(1.2, 53.99) = 118.76, p < 0.001, η²ₚ = 0.725

claims.claim: The authors conclude that vowel hyperarticulation combined with other acoustic-phonetic features enhances comprehensibility, supporting the H&H (Hyper- and Hypoarticulation) theory that speakers modify speech to maximize discriminability for listeners.
claims.quote: vowel hyperarticulation used together with other acoustic–phonetic features in speech to foreign-sounding listeners has an enhancing effect on the comprehensibility in foreigner-directed speech.

claims.claim: FastSpeech uses a length regulator that expands the phoneme hidden sequence according to predicted per-phoneme duration to match the mel-spectrogram length, and speaking rate can be controlled by scaling these durations proportionally.
claims.quote: The length regulator expands the hidden sequence of phonemes according to the duration in order to match the length of a mel-spectrogram sequence.

claims.claim: Speaking rate is natively controllable in the neural model by multiplying phoneme durations, allowing voice speed to vary from 0.5x to 1.5x without loss of voice quality (i.e., native rate generation rather than post-hoc time-stretching).
claims.quote: varying speed from 0.5x to 1.5x without loss of voice quality

claims.claim: The same length-regulator mechanism allows independent control of inter-word breaks by separately adjusting the duration of blank/silence tokens, giving control over pause/rhythm structure.
claims.quote: We can increase or decrease the phoneme duration proportionally to adjust the voice speed and can also change the duration of blank tokens to adjust the break between words.

claims.claim: FastSpeech achieves voice quality comparable to autoregressive models (MOS near Tacotron 2 and Transformer TTS) while the non-autoregressive design dramatically lowers latency, speeding up mel-spectrogram generation ~270x and end-to-end synthesis ~38x, which matters for local/on-device inference.
claims.quote: FastSpeech speeds up the mel-spectrogram generation by about 270 times and speeds up the end-to-end audio synthesis by about 38 times

claims.claim: The non-autoregressive FastSpeech design substantially improves robustness, reducing word skipping/repetition errors to near zero versus a 34% error rate for autoregressive Transformer TTS on difficult sentences, relevant to consistency/predictability at high rates.
claims.quote: Reduced error rate to near-zero versus 34% for Transformer TTS on difficult sentences

claims.claim: Speech intelligibility is better predicted by cochlea-scaled spectral entropy (potential information / unpredictability across time) than by linguistic categories such as consonants, vowels, or their transitions, or by duration.
claims.quote: Speech intelligibility is better predicted by nonlinguistic sensory measures of uncertainty (potential information) than by orthodox physical acoustic measures or linguistic constructs

claims.claim: When portions of speech were replaced by noise, intelligibility decreased linearly with the amount of cochlea-scaled entropy (CSE) replaced, and CSE was a far stronger predictor (r-squared = 0.80) than proportion of vowels replaced (r-squared = 0.55, nonsignificant).
claims.quote: Intelligibility decreased linearly as the amount of replaced CSE increased

claims.claim: Consonant-vowel and vowel categories did not consistently predict relative intelligibility; replacing whole consonants vs vowels did not track performance as phonetic theory predicts.
claims.quote: Relative intelligibility was not well accounted for on the basis of Cs, Vs, or their transitions

claims.claim: CSE was operationalized as Euclidean spectral-change distances between 16 ms slices passed through 33 ERB-scaled auditory filters spanning 26-7743 Hz, i.e. a cochlea-scaled measure of frame-to-frame spectral change.
claims.quote: the extent to which successive spectral slices differ (or cannot be predicted) from preceding spectral slices

claims.claim: The auditory/perceptual system responds primarily to spectral change rather than steady-state segments, implying that the high-information, intelligibility-bearing content is concentrated at moments of rapid spectral transition.
claims.quote: perceptual systems respond primarily to change in the interest of maximizing transmission of new information

claims.claim: Linearly (artificially) time-compressed normal speech is MORE intelligible and faster to process than naturally produced fast speech at the same rate; naturally produced fast words were less intelligible than linearly compressed words and required more processing time at 1.5x normal rate.
claims.quote: Even when naturally produced fast speech is perfectly intelligible, its less careful articulation, combined with the changed timing pattern, slows down processing, relative to linearly time-compressed speech.

claims.claim: Making artificially time-compressed speech mimic the non-linear timing pattern of natural fast speech does NOT help comprehension; it slowed word processing relative to simple uniform linear compression.
claims.quote: word processing speed was slowed down, relative to linear compression

claims.claim: Listeners subjectively prefer artificially (linearly) time-compressed speech over naturally produced fast speech.
claims.quote: listeners preferred artificially time-compressed speech over naturally produced fast speech

claims.claim: Linear time-compression of careful normal-rate speech holds both a temporal and a segmental (articulation-clarity) advantage over fast speech produced naturally by a speaker, because natural fast speech involves less careful articulation and an altered (non-uniform) timing pattern.
claims.quote: linearly time-compressed speech has both a temporal and a segmental advantage over natural fast speech

claims.claim: Native Lombard speech generation via controllable style embeddings substantially reduces word error rate (WER) in noisy conditions compared to normal speech, e.g. at SNR=1 the WER drops from 14.34% (normal) to 6.52% (very loud/Lombard) and at SNR=5 from 7.30% to 3.67%.

The method conditions/fine-tunes an existing neural TTS model (F5-TTS base) using FiLM conditioning and PCA-manipulated style embeddings rather than training from scratch, enabling zero-shot Lombard synthesis and direct prosody control without additional Lombard training data for each new speaker.
claims.quote: This framework enables zero-shot Lombard speech synthesis, generalization to unseen speakers, and direct control over prosody without additional Lombard data.

claims.claim: Improved clarity is achieved through native generation with duration control (adjusting the syllable-to-time ratio) rather than post-hoc time compression of normal speech.
claims.quote: we adjust the total duration of generated speech by modifying the syllable-to-time ratio, leveraging the F5-TTS duration control to enhance perceived clarity

claims.claim: A subjective listening study showed a +0.87 mean CMOS preference for the synthesized Lombard TTS over ground-truth on intelligibility, indicating perceptual intelligibility gains beyond objective WER.
claims.quote: On intelligibility, +0.87 mean preference for TTS Lombard vs. ground-truth across conditions.

claims.claim: Lombard/clear-speech intelligibility correlates with specific acoustic-prosodic properties: increased intensity/loudness (tied to sound pressure level), expanded/diverse formant space, and slower, more articulated production (captured by a clarity principal component).
claims.quote: increased intensity and clearer articulation" with "higher diversity in the formant space ... second principal component (PC2) strongly correlates with clarity," associated with "slower" speech and "more articulated" production

claims.claim: Blind/screen-reader power users prefer voices that are robotic-sounding because they prioritize speed, clarity, predictability, and efficiency over naturalness, the opposite of sighted users' preferences.
claims.quote: blind users tend to prefer voices that are fast, clear, predictable, and efficient. This results in a preference among blind users for voices that sound somewhat robotic

claims.claim: Experienced blind listeners comprehend speech at roughly 800-900 words per minute, about 4x the average human speaking rate of 200-250 wpm, supporting the need for synthesizers optimized for extreme speed.
claims.quote: often upwards of 800 to 900 words per minute. The speaking rate of an average person hovers around 200 to 250 words per minute, for comparison.

claims.claim: Neural TTS models (Supertonic, Kitten TTS) cannot stream incrementally because they require an entire text chunk before generating, whereas screen readers need speech to begin immediately, a latency/responsiveness disadvantage versus formant synthesizers.
claims.quote: Supertonic and Kitten TTS cannot begin generating speech until they have an entire chunk of text... for use in a screen reader, a text to speech system needs to begin generating speech as quickly as possible, rather than waiting for an entire phrase or sentence.

claims.claim: Screen reader users frequently interrupt and jump through text, requiring the TTS to discard and restart speech instantly, a real-time responsiveness requirement neural models struggle to meet.
claims.quote: Users of screen readers quickly jump through text and frequently interrupt the screen reader, and thus require the text to speech system to be able to quickly discard and restart speech.

claims.claim: Older formant synthesizers expose granular voice parameters (pitch, speed, volume, breathiness, roughness, headsize) that neural TTS models cannot, because neural models inherit fixed prosody from training data.
claims.quote: Older text to speech systems make changing the pitch, speed, volume, breathiness, roughness, headsize, and other parameters of the voice easy... AI text to speech models... cannot offer this customization.

claims.claim: Combining Lombard speaking-style fine-tuning with SSDRC signal processing (Lombard-SSDRC TTS) yields large objective intelligibility gains over a normal-speech TTS baseline: 110-130% relative improvement in speech-shaped noise and 47-140% in competing-speaker noise on the SIIB-Gauss metric.
claims.quote: the proposed Lombard-SSDRC TTS shows significant intelligibility gains between 110% and 130% in SSN, and 47% to 140% in CSN against TTS.

claims.claim: In subjective listening tests, the combined Lombard-SSDRC TTS raised the median keyword-correct rate by 455% in speech-shaped noise and 104% in competing-speaker noise relative to the baseline normal TTS, confirming the objective metric trend with human listeners.
claims.quote: Lombard-SSDRC TTS shows superior performance by achieving a remarkable relative improvement of 455% for SSN and 104% for CSN in median keyword correction rate compared to TTS method.

claims.claim: Lombard-style adaptation (training/fine-tuning) and SSDRC signal processing each contribute distinct, additive intelligibility gains: SSDRC TTS already improves over Lombard TTS, and stacking both produces the largest gains, indicating native style fine-tuning and post-hoc DSP are complementary rather than redundant.
claims.quote: Motivating by the boosting effect of Lombard style, along with the enhancement by SSDRC data in terms of speech intelligibility, the proposed Lombard-SSDRC TTS shows significant intelligibility gains

claims.claim: Lombard-style TTS can be successfully fine-tuned via transfer learning with very little target-style data (only ~500 Lombard utterances / ~30 minutes used in training), making clear-speech adaptation feasible without large corpora.
claims.quote: the Lombard speech data has 720 utterances (∼30 minutes). During training, we always consider 2400 utterances for normal and 500 utterances for Lombard speech.

claims.claim: The choice of neural vocoder materially affects intelligibility: swapping WaveNet for WaveRNN in the Lombard adaptation gave 8-12% (SSN) and 15-36% (CSN) relative SIIB-Gauss improvement over the prior baseline Lombard TTS, and 136% (SSN) / 16% (CSN) in median keyword-correct rate.
claims.quote: the proposed Lombard TTS i.e., Lombard TTS (ours) is able to successfully mimic the Lombardness and outperforms baseline Lombard TTS from [14] with a relative improvement between 8% and 12% in SSN and 15% to 36% in CSN

claims.claim: Enhancing the low-frequency temporal modulations of speech by reallocating energy from louder to quieter parts of the signal increases intelligibility in noise — this is the mechanistic basis for modulation-envelope/clear-speech DSP enhancement and is portable across engines because it operates on the output waveform.
claims.quote: DMOD ... enhances the temporal modulations of speech which are a key aspect of speech perception [19],[20],[21] and intelligibility [22], and hence significantly increases word recognition in noise. Rather than using static input-output curves ... DMOD uses a mathematical equation to increase the energy of quieter parts compared to louder parts of speech ... This technique increases the low-frequency temporal modulations of speech and thus significantly improves intelligibility.

claims.claim: Modulation/temporal-envelope enhancement can run in real-time at very low latency (approximately 2 ms processing delay), making it compatible with on-device/streaming pipelines rather than requiring whole-file offline processing.
claims.quote: RMOD is inspired by the DMOD [12], however it performs real-time energy reallocation (approx 2ms delay) and uses novel scaling operations to maintain speech quality.

claims.claim: There is a measurable trade-off between intelligibility gain and speech quality in envelope-enhancement DSP: the more aggressive SSDRC achieved higher intelligibility gains in all tested cases but degraded quality (negative subjective preference), whereas the gentler RMOD preserved quality but only approached SSDRC's intelligibility.
claims.quote: the intelligibility gains of RMOD approach those of SSDRC for both the competing speaker and the canteen noise, although the SSDRC has higher intelligibility gains in all cases ... RMOD and unmodified speech appear to have similar preference scores, whereas SSDRC gives negative quality scores.

claims.claim: Intelligibility gains from envelope/spectral-shaping DSP enhancement are substantially smaller under realistic recorded noise than under synthetic speech-shaped noise, so benefits measured in idealized lab conditions overstate real-world gains.
claims.quote: Results suggest that the intelligibility gains for both algorithms are quite low when tested with real noises. This contrasts with previous results obtained for SSDRC on SSN and demonstrates the importance of testing speech enhancement algorithms in wide range of realistic conditions.

claims.claim: Intelligibility-enhancement DSP should be evaluated with both objective metrics (STOI, extended Speech Intelligibility Index, Glimpse Portion, POLQA) and subjective preference/listening tests, because some metrics capture intelligibility only while others jointly account for quality and distortion.
claims.quote: Several objective measures of intelligibility were used ... the extended Speech Intelligibility Index (extSII,[25]), the Glimpse Portion model (GP,[26]), the Distortion Weighted Glimpse Portion model (DWGP,[4]) and the Short-Time Objective Intelligibility measure (STOI,[27]). While GP and extSII assess intelligibility, DWGP and STOI are correlated with both intelligibility and quality.

claims.claim: Blind screen-reader power users prefer voices that are fast, clear, predictable, and efficient, which results in a preference for somewhat robotic-sounding voices that remain understandable at very high speeds, directly trading naturalness for intelligibility.
claims.quote: blind users tend to prefer voices that are fast, clear, predictable, and efficient. This results in a preference among blind users for voices that sound somewhat robotic, but can be understood at high rates of speed

claims.claim: Screen-reader power users commonly listen at 800-900 words per minute, roughly 3-4x the average human speaking rate of 200-250 wpm, establishing the extreme-speed regime where formant synthesizers are favored.
claims.quote: often upwards of 800 to 900 words per minute

claims.claim: Modern neural/AI TTS systems are designed to sound human, natural, and conversational, but this naturalness comes at the expense of accuracy/intelligibility -- a core argument for why power listeners reject them.
claims.quote: These modern systems are developed to sound human, natural, and conversational. Unfortunately this seems to come at the expense of accuracy.

claims.claim: Latency/responsiveness is a decisive factor: screen reader users rapidly skip and interrupt speech, so the engine must begin producing audio immediately and restart instantly, whereas neural engines like Supertonic and Kitten TTS cannot start until a full text chunk is available.
claims.quote: Supertonic and Kitten TTS cannot begin generating speech until they have an entire chunk of text.

claims.claim: Older formant synthesizers expose granular, real-time control over pitch, speed, volume, breathiness, roughness, and head size, while neural TTS trained on fixed speakers cannot offer this parameter customization -- limiting tunability for speed optimization.
claims.quote: AI text to speech models, being trained on data from a particular set of speakers, cannot offer this customization.

claims.claim: Trained blind listeners can comprehend speech at up to about 22 syllables/second, far exceeding the ~8 syllables/second maximum of untrained listeners.
claims.quote: understanding spoken language at a rate of up to about 22 syllables (syl) per seconds (s)—exceeding by far the maximum performance level of untrained listeners (ca. 8 syl/s)

claims.claim: The ultra-fast speech stimuli used by these blind power listeners were generated with the formant synthesizer 'Eloquence' running in the JAWS screen reader, directly evidencing the blind community's reliance on formant synthesis at high speed.
claims.quote: formant synthesizer 'eloquence' implemented in the synthesizer JAWS

claims.claim: Before training, comprehension of 18 syl/s ultra-fast speech was near zero (correctly reproduced syllables below 14%), but after ~6 months of training four of six participants reached about 70 percentage points correct, demonstrating a large measurable trainability effect.
claims.quote: Four of six participants showed—independently from residual visual functions—considerable enhancement of ultra-fast speech perception (about 70% points correctly repeated words)

claims.claim: Prior to training, all participants performed below 14% correct on ultra-fast (18 syl/s) speech, quantifying the baseline difficulty of comprehension at extreme rates without adaptation.
claims.quote: the percentage of correctly reproduced syllables fell below 14% in all instances

claims.claim: At a moderately fast rate of 8 syl/s, comprehension remained near ceiling (mean ~97%), indicating that intelligibility breakdown is rate-dependent and emerges only at extreme syllable rates well above normal speech.
claims.quote: At 8 syl/s pre-training: "mean = 97%, SE = 6.94%"

claims.claim: The STMI is a biologically-motivated speech-intelligibility metric that uses an auditory model to analyze the joint spectro-temporal modulations in speech and a channel's ability to transmit them; a clean signal yields a characteristic spectro-temporal modulation (STM) profile that distortions (noise, reverberation) degrade.
claims.quote: The method employs an auditory model to analyze the effects of noise, reverberations, and other distortions on the joint spectro-temporal modulations present in speech, and the ability of a channel to transmit these modulations. The effects are summarized by a spectro-temporal modulation index (STMI).

claims.claim: STMI captures difficult, nonlinear distortions such as phase jitter and spectral shifts to which the classical Speech Transmission Index (STI) is insensitive — i.e., STMI drops as phase jitter increases while STI fails to register the degradation.
claims.quote: The STMI can handle difficult and nonlinear distortions such as phase-jitter and shifts, to which the STI is not sensitive. The results show that the STMI drops as phase jitter increases, while STI fails to capture the presence of noise in this channel.

claims.claim: The STMI was validated by comparing its predictions both to the classical STI and to human listener error rates for speech contaminated with combined noise and reverberation.
claims.quote: The index is validated by comparing its predictions to those of the classical STI and to error rates reported by human subjects listening to speech contaminated with combined noise and reverberation.

claims.claim: The paper is peer-reviewed primary research: Elhilali, Chi & Shamma, 'A spectro-temporal modulation index (STMI) for assessment of speech intelligibility,' Speech Communication vol. 41 (2-3), pp. 331-348, October 2003, DOI 10.1016/S0167-6393(02)00134-6.
claims.quote: A spectro-temporal modulation index (STMI) for assessment of speech intelligibility ... M. Elhilali, T. Chi, S. A. Shamma ... Speech Communication ... pages 331-348 ... doi=10.1016/S0167-6393(02)00134-6

claims.claim: Highly trained blind listeners can comprehend speech at rates up to ~22 syllables/second, roughly 2.5x to 3x the maximum rate normal-sighted listeners can follow (~8 syl/s). This validates that extreme speed-listening (the target of an aggressively speed-optimized track) is a learnable, real perceptual capability concentrated in a trained power-listener population.
claims.quote: blind people...may learn to understand spoken language at a rate of up to about 22 syllables (syl) per second...exceeding by far the maximum performance level of normal-sighted listeners (ca. 8 syl/s)

claims.claim: At 16 syllables/second (ultra-fast condition), blind subjects' comprehension ranged from 0 to 93% (mean 59.7%, SD 24.0%), whereas sighted controls consistently fell below 16% (mean 9.0%, SD 4.7%). This quantifies a massive trained-listener vs untrained gap at ultra-fast rates and confirms ~16 syl/s as a meaningful design ceiling distinguishing the two intended use cases (general students vs trained power listeners).
claims.quote: extended...from 0 to 93% (mean = 59.7%, SD ± 24.0%)... performance level consistently fell below 16% (mean = 9.0%, SD ± 4.7%)

claims.claim: Ultra-fast speech comprehension in proficient blind listeners engages non-auditory brain regions, specifically right primary visual cortex (V1), contralateral fusiform gyrus, and bilateral pulvinar, alongside classic language regions (left inferior frontal gyrus, posterior superior temporal sulcus, supplementary motor area). This indicates the ultra-fast skill relies on cross-modal neural recruitment from training/plasticity rather than just acoustic clarity of the signal.
claims.quote: blind people highly proficient in ultra-fast speech perception showed significant hemodynamic activation of right-hemispheric primary visual cortex (V1), contralateral fusiform gyrus (FG), and bilateral pulvinar (Pv)

claims.claim: Activation of recruited regions (including V1, r = .533, p < .05) correlated positively with individual ultra-fast comprehension ability, meaning the capacity scales with listener-specific neural adaptation. This implies comprehensibility at extreme speed is substantially listener-trained rather than a fixed property of the TTS signal, bounding how much a speed-optimized voice alone can deliver to untrained users.
claims.quote: all ROIs showed a significant positive trend towards stronger hemodynamic activation under the ultra-fast speech condition in case of enhanced ultra-fast speech perception capabilities (V1: r = .533, p < .05)
