claims.claim: For normal-hearing listeners, the dominant acoustic modification driving the clear-speech intelligibility gain in fricatives is a shift of spectral energy toward higher frequency regions, evidenced by significant positive correlations between intelligibility advantage and DFT/LPC spectral-peak location and spectral moment 1 (M1).
claims.quote: Significant positive correlations (p<0.05) were obtained between intelligibility advantages and acoustic modifications in DFT and LPC peak location and spectral moment 1 (M1)... These results suggest that a shift of spectral energy to higher frequency regions in clear fricatives is most closely related to the overall intelligibility enhancement.

claims.claim: The clear-speech intelligibility benefit is not uniform across consonant types: sibilant fricatives gain more from clear speech than non-sibilant fricatives, and the clear-speech advantage failed to reach significance (or even reversed) for some non-sibilant pairs such as /f/-/th/.
claims.quote: A Style × Sibilance interaction (p<.01) showed that, while both sibilants and non-sibilants were more intelligible in clear speech, the effect was larger for sibilant pairs.

claims.claim: The acoustic strategy that improves intelligibility is listener-population-dependent: for simulated-hearing-impaired listeners, transposing cues to higher frequencies was less effective or reversed, and benefits came instead from cues in lower-frequency regions, implying intelligibility enhancement must be matched to the listener rather than applied as a single fixed transform.
claims.quote: Thus, results tended in all cases in the opposite direction of Experiment 1; the more clear speech modifications involved higher-frequency acoustic information, the less effective they were for (simulated) hearing impaired listeners.

claims.claim: Beyond the high-frequency energy shift, greater source strength (frication amplitude/energy) was identified as a contributing acoustic correlate of the clear-speech benefit for normal-hearing listeners, indicating that boosting consonant source energy is one lever for intelligibility enhancement.
claims.quote: Correlation analyses comparing acoustic and perceptual data indicated that a shift of energy concentration toward higher frequency regions and greater source strength contributed to the clear speech effect for normal-hearing listeners

claims.claim: Trained blind (vision-impaired) listeners can learn to comprehend accelerated synthetic speech at rates up to approximately 22 syllables per second, far exceeding the ~8 syllables/second upper limit of normal sighted listeners.
claims.quote: vision-impaired individuals may learn to understand accelerated spoken language at a rate of up to about 22 syllables (syl) per second (s) ... exceeding by far the capacities of normal sighted listeners whose upper limit is at ca. 8 syl/s

claims.claim: The ultra-fast speech-listening experiments with blind subjects used the Eloquence formant synthesizer (via the JAWS screen reader), the same class of formant synthesizer that blind screen-reader power users favor for high-speed listening.
claims.quote: screen-reader JAWS (male voice, synthesizer "Eloquence,"

claims.claim: After training, blind participants reached roughly 70% comprehension at an ultra-fast rate of 18 syllables/second (four of six subjects), whereas before training none could understand 18 syl/s speech (below 14% syllables correctly reproduced).
claims.quote: none of the subjects was able to understand ultra-fast speech at a level of 18 syl/s. More specifically, the percentage of correctly reproduced syllables fell below 14% in all instances.

claims.claim: Sighted control listeners could not match blind listeners at high rates, with sighted performance falling consistently below 20% comprehension at the fast rates tested (e.g., 16 syl/s).
claims.quote: the performance level of sighted subjects fell consistently below 20%

claims.claim: The study trained blind participants at three discrete acceleration rates — moderately fast (8 syl/s), intermediate (13 syl/s), and ultra-fast (18 syl/s) — establishing that comprehensibility at speed is a trainable skill rather than a fixed perceptual limit.
claims.quote: vision-impaired individuals may learn to understand accelerated spoken language at a rate of up to about 22 syllables (syl) per second (s)

claims.claim: Blind listeners in a referenced study could comprehend speech at rates up to ~25 syllables per second, far beyond the sighted limit.
claims.quote: Blind people in the study could hear speech as fast as 25 syllables a second!

claims.claim: Sighted listeners max out at much lower speech rates than blind power-listeners, with a sighted baseline around 14 syllables/second versus 24+ for blind users.
claims.quote: I am sighted and max out already at the 14 sps sample

claims.claim: Blind screen-reader users find formant synthesizers easier to comprehend when sped up than concatenative (neural/natural) synthesizers.
claims.quote: I, and I know I'm not the only one, find formant synths easier to comprehend when speeded up than concatenated ones

claims.claim: In blindness, auditory processing regions of the brain get re-wired to recruit the cerebral cortex that normally handles vision, supporting ultrafast speech comprehension.
claims.quote: The parts of the brain that process hearing get re-wired to the part of the cerebral cortex that normally handles vision

claims.claim: Time-scale modification that speeds up speech without changing pitch is used by talking-book players to make ultrafast narration intelligible.
claims.quote: My digital talking book player allows me to speed up the voices of the human narrators without changing the pitch

claims.claim: There is a divide between novice and expert screen-reader users in voice preference: novices prioritize voice naturalness while experts care little about TTS quality and instead prioritize reliability and application support.
claims.quote: Advanced users of screen readers cited little interest in TTS quality and instead voiced much more concern about TTS reliability and application support.

claims.claim: Expert users routinely speed up TTS output, which makes the voice sound more artificial/robotic, the very quality novices dislike, indicating that at high speed naturalness is sacrificed and is no longer the priority for trained listeners.
claims.quote: expert users typically speed up the audio output which causes the output to sound more artificial, precisely the quality that novice users tended to dislike about eSpeak

claims.claim: Users do not distinguish the TTS voice from the screen reader software and develop early lock-in to a specific voice, with only 13% of respondents reporting ever switching screen reader software.
claims.quote: only 13% (n = 102) of respondents replied having ever switched the screen reader software

claims.claim: Eloquence (a formant-based synthesizer bundled with JAWS) was the dominant, preferred voice across both desktop (JAWS) and mobile (Nuance Talks) screen readers, perceived as more natural than eSpeak which sounded mechanical.
claims.quote: Most people in India are very familiar with Eloquence, hence Talks is very readily accepted.

claims.claim: Intelligibility/comprehension was not the primary user complaint about eSpeak; complaints centered on it sounding mechanical rather than being hard to understand.
claims.quote: very little discussion on the intelligibility of eSpeak

claims.claim: FastSpeech's length regulator enables native control of speaking rate from 0.5x to 1.5x speed at generation time (not post-hoc time-stretching) without loss of voice quality, by scaling predicted phoneme durations.
claims.quote: FastSpeech can adjust the voice speed through the length regulator, varying speed from 0.5x to 1.5x without loss of voice quality.

claims.claim: FastSpeech can control part of the prosody, specifically inter-word pause/break length, by adjusting the duration of blank tokens at generation time.
claims.quote: can also change the duration of blank tokens to adjust the break between words in order to control part of the prosody.

claims.claim: Native duration-prediction generation eliminates word repeating and skipping errors that plague autoregressive TTS; on hard cases Transformer TTS has a 34% error rate while FastSpeech effectively eliminates them.
claims.quote: Transformer TTS is not robust when it comes to these hard cases and gets a 34% error rate, while FastSpeech can effectively eliminate word repeating and skipping.

claims.claim: FastSpeech achieves large inference speedups: ~270x faster mel-spectrogram generation and ~38x faster end-to-end audio synthesis, supporting feasibility of controllable non-autoregressive TTS for local/fast pipelines.
claims.quote: FastSpeech speeds up the mel-spectrogram generation by about 270 times and speeds up the end-to-end audio synthesis by about 38 times.

claims.claim: Despite non-autoregressive generation with explicit duration control, FastSpeech's voice quality nearly matches autoregressive Transformer TTS and Tacotron 2 (MOS), showing rate-controllable generation need not sacrifice naturalness.
claims.quote: FastSpeech nearly matches the quality of the Transformer TTS model and Tacotron 2

claims.claim: Combining Lombard speaking-style conversion with SSDRC DSP processing (Lombard-SSDRC TTS) yields large objective intelligibility gains over a normal/baseline TTS voice, measured by SIIB-Gauss: 110-130% relative improvement in speech-shaped noise and 47-140% in competing-speaker noise.
claims.quote: the Lombard-SSDRC system showed... Speech-Shaped Noise (SSN): 'significant relative improvement between 110% and 130%'... Competing-Speaker Noise (CSN): '47% to 140%'

claims.claim: Subjective listening tests (keyword correction rate) confirm the Lombard-SSDRC TTS gives very large intelligibility gains over baseline: 455% relative improvement in speech-shaped noise and 104% in competing-speaker noise.
claims.quote: Subjective evaluation (keyword correction rate): SSN: 'relative improvement of 455%' CSN: '104%' (compared to baseline)

claims.claim: SSDRC (Spectral Shaping and Dynamic Range Compression) is a post-hoc DSP technique that improves intelligibility by redistributing signal energy in the time-frequency domain, applicable on top of TTS output regardless of the synthesis engine.
claims.quote: Spectral Shaping and Dynamic Range Compression (SSDRC) which has been shown to provide high intelligibility gains by redistributing the signal energy on the time-frequency domain

claims.claim: The intelligibility-enhanced TTS was built using a Tacotron + WaveRNN neural pipeline with transfer learning to convert plain speech into Lombard style, demonstrating a neural TTS can be conditioned/adapted to natively generate clearer speech.
claims.quote: TTS Architecture: Tacotron and WaveRNN... Approach: Transfer learning

claims.claim: The published study evaluates the combined Lombard-SSDRC system rather than isolating the separate contributions of Lombard style conversion versus SSDRC DSP, so the marginal gain of each component alone is not quantified here.
claims.quote: The paper doesn't isolate Lombard vs. SSDRC individually—results report the combined Lombard-SSDRC system as superior to 'state-of-the-art TTS approach'

claims.claim: A neural TTS conditioned to produce Lombard/clear speech via controllable style embeddings improves intelligibility in noise without needing dedicated Lombard training data, demonstrating that style-conditioning a neural model can natively boost clarity.
claims.quote: The system synthesizes Lombard speech without explicit Lombard training data by manipulating style embeddings learned from prosodically diverse datasets, using PCA to control loudness and clarity.

claims.claim: Increasing 'Lombardness' (clarity/loudness style) in the synthesized speech measurably and consistently reduces word error rate across noise levels; at SNR=5 the Very Loud TTS condition achieved 3.67% WER versus 12.66% for normal ground-truth speech.
claims.quote: Very Loud condition at SNR=5: WER improved from 12.66% (normal GT) to 3.67% (TTS) ... "increasing Lombardness consistently improves intelligibility across noise levels"

claims.claim: Clarity and loudness contribute differently to intelligibility: clarity is essential for intelligibility generally while loudness dominates in high-noise environments, indicating these prosodic dimensions can be independently controlled for comprehensibility gains.
claims.quote: The ablation study showed clarity is "essential for intelligibility" while loudness had "dominant effect in high-noise environments," quantified through WER divergence across conditions.

claims.claim: There is a measurable naturalness-versus-intelligibility trade-off: subjective CMOS listening tests showed the Lombard TTS was preferred for intelligibility in clean conditions (+1.48) but rated lower than ground truth for overall naturalness (-1.22).
claims.quote: Intelligibility (Clean): +1.48±0.38 favoring TTS ... Overall naturalness: −1.22±0.26 (ground truth slightly preferred)

claims.claim: The style-control method preserves speaker identity while applying clarity/loudness shifts, maintaining roughly 81% absolute speaker similarity (SSIM) across all Lombardness levels.
claims.quote: Absolute SSIM: ~81% across all Lombardness levels

claims.claim: Recognition scores for time-compressed (TC) speech systematically over-estimate recognition of naturally-produced fast-rate speech; listeners do substantially worse on natural fast speech than on time-compressed speech at a comparable rate, so a TC voice does not behave like genuinely fast speech.
claims.quote: Recognition of time-compressed sentences over-estimated recognition of natural fast sentences for both groups, especially for older listeners.

claims.claim: The intelligibility ranking from best to worst is normal-rate, then 40% time-compressed, then natural fast speech (statistically significant, p <= 0.006), establishing that uniform time-compression is more intelligible than naturally fast speech at matched rate.
claims.quote: performance was best for the normal rate speech, significantly poorer for the 40TC rate, and poorest with natural fast speech

claims.claim: The intelligibility advantage of time-compressed over natural fast speech held across listening conditions (quiet and noise) and sentence types (high- and low-context), making it a robust effect rather than a condition-specific artifact.
claims.quote: Both listener groups consistently exhibited significantly poorer recognition scores for natural fast-rate speech than for TC speech... This was true in quiet and noise, and for both high-context and low-context sentences.

claims.claim: Natural fast speech degrades intelligibility because it introduces segmental distortions (deletions and distortions of stops/affricates from co-articulation and lenition/target undershoot) that uniform time-scale compression does not introduce, since TC preserves spectral information and temporal fine structure at a reduced time scale.
claims.quote: Preliminary acoustic analyses of the natural fast recordings in this study show not only shorter overall phoneme durations, but also deletions and distortions, especially of stops and affricates.

claims.claim: SSDRC enhances speech intelligibility in noise by cascading two stages: spectral shaping (frequency-domain enhancement) followed by dynamic range compression (time-domain enhancement), under a constraint of equal global signal power before and after modification.
claims.quote: The signal is enhanced before presented in a noisy environment, under the constraint of equal global signal power before and after modifications. Two systems are combined in a cascade form to enhance the quality of the signal first in frequency (spectral shaping) and then in time (dynamic range compression).

claims.claim: SSDRC yields an equivalent SNR-gain intelligibility improvement of 4 dB in speech-shaped noise (SSN) and 8 dB in competing-speaker (CS) noise at low SNR values.
claims.quote: In terms of SNR gain there is an improvement of 4 dB (SSN) and 8 dB (CS).

claims.claim: The SSDRC spectral-shaping-plus-dynamic-range-compression approach outperforms state-of-the-art intelligibility-enhancement methods on objective measures (Speech Intelligibility Index) and in formal listening tests.
claims.quote: Experiments with speech shaped (SSN) and competed speaker (CS) types of noise at various low SNR values, show that the suggested approach outperforms state-of-the art methods

claims.claim: The intelligibility enhancement is achieved using a non-parametric, power-preserving signal modification, meaning gains come from redistributing existing signal energy across frequency and time rather than increasing overall loudness.
claims.quote: we suggest a non-parametric way to improve the intelligibility of speech in noise ... under the constraint of equal global signal power before and after modifications

claims.claim: Blind people who train in ultra-fast speech comprehension can understand spoken language at up to ~22 syllables per second, roughly 2.5-3x the maximum rate of normal-sighted listeners (~8 syl/s).
claims.quote: Individuals suffering from vision loss...may learn to understand spoken language at a rate of up to about 22 syllables (syl) per second - exceeding by far the maximum performance level of normal-sighted listeners (ca. 8 syl/s).

claims.claim: Ultra-fast speech comprehension in blind subjects is a trained/learned skill rather than an innate capacity, implying high-speed-listening ability develops with practice.
claims.quote: Individuals suffering from vision loss...may learn to understand spoken language at a rate of up to about 22 syllables (syl) per second

claims.claim: In proficient blind listeners, ultra-fast (16 syl/s) speech recruits non-auditory brain regions including primary visual cortex (V1), fusiform gyrus, and bilateral pulvinar, indicating cross-modal reorganization underlies the speed advantage.
claims.quote: blind people highly proficient in ultra-fast speech perception showed significant hemodynamic activation of right-hemispheric primary visual cortex (V1), contralateral fusiform gyrus (FG), and bilateral pulvinar (Pv).

claims.claim: The pulvinar together with auditory cortex and visual cortex is proposed to implement a signal-driven timing mechanism tied to the syllabic (suprasegmental) modulation of the speech signal, suggesting syllable-rate temporal envelope structure is the bottleneck at high speed.
claims.quote: the collaboration of bilateral pulvinar, right auditory cortex, and ipsilateral V1 implements a signal-driven timing mechanism related to syllabic (suprasegmental) modulation of the speech signal.

claims.claim: The fMRI study tested two syllabic rates, a moderately fast 8 syl/s and an ultra-fast 16 syl/s condition, in 14 blind (3 early-blind, 11 late-blind) and 12 sighted subjects.
claims.quote: the study tested two conditions: Moderately fast: 8 syl/s ... Ultra-fast: 16 syl/s ... 3 early-blind individuals and 11 late-blind participants (n=14 total blind subjects vs. 12 sighted controls)

claims.claim: A UNIFORM (linear, equal-across-all-segments) time-compression algorithm produced clearly better speech intelligibility than a NON-UNIFORM (segment-dependent) algorithm for speech-in-noise testing, contradicting the intuition that selectively compressing steady-state segments helps; the non-uniform algorithm deviated more from target compression and distorted phoneme duration, spectra, and modulation spectra more.
claims.quote: Subjective and objective measures indicated a clear advantage of the uniform algorithm in comparison to the non-uniform algorithm for the application in speech-in-noise tests.

claims.claim: The non-uniform (content-aware, segment-dependent) compression approach introduced larger acoustic distortions than uniform compression — specifically greater deviations from the targeted compression ratio and greater changes to phoneme duration, spectra, and the modulation spectrum.
claims.quote: exhibited greater deviations from the targeted time compression, as well as greater changes of the phoneme duration, spectra, and modulation spectra

claims.claim: Speech intelligibility degrades sharply only at extreme compression: with 48 normal-hearing listeners, speech had to be compressed to more than 30% of its original length (i.e., faster than ~3.3x speed) before intelligibility dropped to the 50% threshold at positive signal-to-noise ratios.
claims.quote: speech had to be compressed to more than 30% of its original length to reach 50% intelligibility at positive [signal-to-noise ratios]

claims.claim: The study used time-compressed sentences from the German Oldenburg Sentence Test (OLSA) at varying speech rates to parametrically increase test difficulty while attempting to preserve relevant acoustical speech cues for a hearing-aid algorithm assessment method.
claims.quote: The research used compressed versions of the German Oldenburg Sentence Test at varying speech rates to establish a hearing aid assessment method that would shift test thresholds to practical signal-to-noise ratios for hearing-impaired listeners while preserving acoustical speech cues.

claims.claim: Linearly time-compressed normal-rate speech is processed faster and is preferred by listeners over naturally produced fast speech, even when both are perfectly intelligible — i.e., the natural-speech 'reduction' that occurs when a human speaks fast is a net liability, not an asset. This directly undercuts the assumption that training a TTS to natively produce human-style fast speech would beat simply time-compressing a normal voice.
claims.quote: Even when naturally produced fast speech is perfectly intelligible, its less careful articulation, combined with the changed timing pattern, slows down processing, relative to linearly time-compressed speech. Furthermore, listeners preferred artificially time-compressed speech over naturally produced fast speech. These results suggest that linearly time-compressed speech has both a temporal and a segmental advantage over natural fast speech.

claims.claim: The processing advantage of linear time-compression over natural fast speech is quantified: in a phoneme-detection task, mean detection time was 572 ms for linear compression vs. 624 ms for natural fast speech — a statistically significant 52 ms advantage (copy-fast-speech-timing was intermediate at 600 ms).
claims.quote: detection times are fastest in the linear compression condition (572 ms), followed by the copy-fast-speech-timing (600 ms), and the natural fast speech condition (624 ms) ... Only the 52 ms advantage of linear compression over natural fast speech is significant.

claims.claim: Making artificially time-compressed speech imitate the non-linear timing pattern of natural fast speech (compressing unstressed syllables more than stressed ones — e.g. stressed to 65%, unstressed to 45%) SLOWS word processing rather than improving it; prior work found it actually hurt intelligibility relative to plain linear compression. So investing in prosody-mimicking 'rate-aware' generation that copies human fast-speech timing is likely wasted effort.
claims.quote: word processing speed was slowed down, relative to linear compression ... for this heavily time-compressed speech, making the timing pattern more similar to that of natural fast speech turned out to have a negative effect on intelligibility, relative to linear time compression (Janse et al., 2003).

claims.claim: The single aspect of natural fast speech worth imitating for high-speed intelligibility is disproportionate compression of pauses (compress silences more than the speech itself); pause-reduction algorithms such as Mach1 measurably beat uniform linear time-compression at fast playback rates. This is a concrete, cheap DSP recommendation that applies to any engine's output.
claims.quote: The only aspect of naturally produced fast speech that should be imitated in order to make time-compressed speech more intelligible may be compression of pause duration. The results obtained with the Mach1 algorithm (Covell et al., 1998) and with other pause-reduction algorithms (He and Gupta, 2001) have shown that by compressing pauses more than the remaining speech, intelligibility can be improved over linear time compression at fast playback rates.

claims.claim: Segmental redundancy and careful, near-canonical articulation are what make fast speech easier to recognise; increased coarticulation/assimilation (which natural fast human speech introduces) blurs phoneme boundaries and reduces signal redundancy, harming perception. This supports favouring a crisp/hyperarticulated voice as the source for a speed track rather than a relaxed conversational one.
claims.quote: Assimilation and increased coarticulation reduce the redundancy of the speech signal, and they blur segmental distinctions. Segmental redundancy is beneficial for perception of fast speech ... the less words deviate from their normal-rate or ÔcanonicalÕ form, the easier it is for the listener to map the incoming information onto the mental lexicon.

result.publishDate: 2004 (Speech Communication, vol. 42, pp. 155-173; received 25 July 2002, accepted 29 July 2003)

claims.claim: Speech intelligibility is best predicted by cochlea-scaled spectral change across time (cochlea-scaled entropy, CSE) rather than by consonants, vowels, or segment duration — meaning regions of greatest spectral change, not specific phoneme categories, carry the most intelligibility-critical information.
claims.quote: We report two experiments that demonstrate listener performance to be better predicted by simple psychoacoustic measures of cochlea-scaled spectral change across time.

claims.claim: When sentence segments are replaced by noise, intelligibility tracks the amount of cochlea-scaled entropy replaced with a strong linear fit (r-squared = 0.80, P < 0.01), substantially outperforming the proportion of vowels replaced, which is a nonsignificant predictor (r-squared = 0.55).
claims.quote: Intelligibility closely follows measures of entropy replaced (linear regression fit: r² = 0.80, P < 0.01) ... the proportion of vowels replaced is a nonsignificant predictor of intelligibility (r² = 0.55).

claims.claim: Replacing low-entropy (steady-state, predictable) segments has relatively small impact on intelligibility, while replacing high-entropy (high spectral-change) segments significantly reduces intelligibility — directly implying that steady-state vowel regions can be compressed/degraded more safely than dynamic spectral transitions.
claims.quote: Intelligibility decreased linearly as the amount of replaced CSE increased. Duration of signal replaced and proportion of consonants/vowels replaced fail to account for listener data.

claims.claim: Cochlea-scaled entropy is operationalised as Euclidean distances between ERB-scaled spectra of fixed 16 ms slices — a concrete, computable psychoacoustic measure of how much each spectral slice differs from preceding slices, usable as an objective intelligibility-relevant metric.
claims.quote: Euclidean distances between equivalent-rectangular-bandwidth-scaled spectra of fixed-duration (16 ms) sentence slices.

claims.claim: The apparent 'vowel superiority' for intelligibility is an artifact of vowels being roughly one-third longer than the replaced consonant segments, not of vowels being inherently more informative — so duration confounds must be controlled when reasoning about which segments to preserve under time compression.
claims.quote: such conclusions are mitigated by the fact that replaced consonant segments were roughly one-third shorter than vowels.

claims.claim: Crowdsourced MUSHRA tests on Prolific reproduce expert-lab codec rankings and closely match absolute expert scores (Pearson 0.95, Spearman 0.91), making them a reliable, cheaper alternative to expert-listener tests for evaluating speech quality including generative codecs.
claims.quote: both Prolific and MTurk listeners produce the same overall ranking of codecs as Internal listeners (i.e., “ground truth”). However, Prolific’s scores more closely trace the Internal curve

claims.claim: Crowdsourcing platform choice introduces measurable bias: MTurk listeners show a ceiling effect and drift from expert absolute scores (Pearson 0.89-0.90), while Prolific aligns closely (0.95); reaching stable results required ~15 responses per item on Prolific vs ~25 on MTurk.
claims.quote: For Prolific, we observed stable results with approximately 15 responses for each test item, while MTurk required around 25 responses per test item.

claims.claim: For non-expert crowdsourced MUSHRA, the test should be simplified relative to the ITU-R BS.1534-3 expert protocol: at most 6 conditions in parallel (vs up to 12 for experts), shorter clips (~6.4 s avg vs the 10-12 s expert recommendation), and up to three rating blocks to limit fatigue.
claims.quote: In our experience, a maximum of 6 conditions should not be exceeded in a crowdsourced test.

claims.claim: Rigorous listener screening is essential for valid crowdsourced perceptual tests: pre-screen on task success rate >97% and >=100 completed tasks plus a digits-in-noise hearing test, and disqualify listeners who fail basic checks (anchor rated above hidden reference, or zero rating variance) on more than 20% of questions.
claims.quote: Listeners are disqualified entirely if they fail basic quality checks on more than 20% of the MUSHRA questions in a test block.

claims.claim: The MUSHRA anchor must introduce the same type of degradation as the systems under test; using a mismatched anchor (e.g. low-pass/bandwidth limitation against coding artifacts) made quality harder to judge and produced less consistent ratings for non-expert listeners.
claims.quote: participants found it harder to judge quality because the artifacts were of a different type (bandwidth limitation instead of coding distortion)

claims.claim: Blind/screen-reader power users overwhelmingly prefer Eloquence (a 2003-era formant-style synthesizer) over modern natural voices because their priorities are speed, clarity, predictability, and efficiency rather than naturalness.
claims.quote: the voice that is preferred by the majority of western English blind users, called Eloquence, was last updated in 2003 ... Sighted users prefer voices that are natural, conversational, and as human-like as possible, blind users tend to prefer voices that are fast, clear, predictable, and efficient.

claims.claim: Screen-reader power users routinely listen at 800-900 words per minute, roughly 3-4x the average speaking rate of 200-250 wpm, which is the speed regime in which the preferred robotic voices remain intelligible.
claims.quote: can be understood at high rates of speed, often upwards of 800 to 900 words per minute. The speaking rate of an average person hovers around 200 to 250 words per minute

claims.claim: The community-preferred high-speed voice is technically obsolete and unmaintainable: Eloquence is a 32-bit binary last compiled in 2003 that requires emulation/bridging on modern systems, has unfixable known security issues, and no one (including Apple) can locate or recompile its source.
claims.quote: Eloquence is a 32-bit voice last compiled in 2003, it cannot run in modern software without some sort of emulation or bridge. ... even large companies like Apple haven't managed to find or compile it. The eloquence libraries also have many known security issues ... Eloquence itself can never be updated or fixed.

claims.claim: The author characterizes preferred high-speed screen-reader voices as sounding robotic yet remaining comprehensible at high speed, framing a naturalness-versus-high-speed-intelligibility trade-off where the robotic option wins for power listeners.
claims.quote: sound somewhat robotic, but can be understood at high rates of speed

claims.claim: Despite rapid advances in AI/neural TTS, blind users have been excluded from these gains and current stopgaps are unsustainable, implying modern neural voices have not yet matched the high-speed intelligibility of legacy synthesizers.
claims.quote: Blind people getting left out of the explosion of text to speech advancement ... These stopgap solutions are entirely untenable, and are likely to take us only so far. A better solution is urgently needed.

claims.claim: Generating speech at a normal speaking rate and then applying linear time-compression produced the MOST intelligible fast synthetic speech at all tested rates (for English voices) — outperforming native fast generation methods (duration-model variance scaling and interpolating fast/normal duration models). This directly bears on the user's core question of whether native fast generation/fine-tuning beats post-hoc time-compression.
claims.quote: Word recognition results for the English voices show that generating speech at a normal speaking rate and then applying linear compression resulted in the most intelligible speech at all tested rates.

claims.claim: Three rate-control methods were compared: (a) scaling the variance of the state duration model, (b) interpolating the duration models of fast and normal voices, and (c) applying linear compression to the generated waveform — i.e., two 'native' fast-generation approaches vs. one post-hoc compression approach. The post-hoc/hybrid linear method was most reliable at very high rates.
claims.quote: Three compression methods were compared: scaling the variance of the state duration model, interpolating the duration models of the fast and the normal voices, and applying a linear compression method to the generated speech waveform... Linear compression can more reliably provide higher intelligibility, particularly at higher rates.

claims.claim: Counterintuitively, 'clear speech' style does NOT survive time-compression as well as normal speech: compressed normal speech was more intelligible than compressed clear speech even when total speech-plus-silence duration was held equal — cautioning against training a fast track on clear-speech corpora without testing.
claims.quote: Even when the overall duration of speech and silence is kept the same across styles, compressed normal speech is still more intelligible than compressed clear speech.

claims.claim: Non-uniform compression of pauses helps: compressing silence/pauses roughly twice as much as the speech segments further improved intelligibility of normal speech at almost no extra computational cost — a cheap DSP-tier technique for the local pipeline.
claims.quote: Compressing silence twice as much as speech improved results further for normal speech with very little additional computational costs.

claims.claim: Results were language-dependent: for German voices, duration-model interpolation was better at moderate rates but linear compression again won at very high rates (especially applied to fast-recorded data), showing the optimal method depends on target rate and language.
claims.quote: For the German voices, interpolation was found to be better at moderate speaking rates but the linear method was again more successful at very high rates, particularly when applied to the fast data.

result.publishDate: 2015-09 (Speech Communication, Vol. 73; Valentini-Botinhao, Toman, Pucher, Yamagishi et al.)

claims.claim: Enhancing the temporal modulations of speech (by reallocating energy from louder to quieter parts via a non-linear compression A'=A^alpha, 0<alpha<1) significantly increases word recognition / intelligibility in noise, because low-frequency temporal modulations are a key aspect of speech perception and intelligibility.
claims.quote: DMOD belongs to the family of humanlike speech modifications; it enhances the temporal modulations of speech which are a key aspect of speech perception [19],[20],[21] and intelligibility [22], and hence significantly increases word recognition in noise... This technique increases the low-frequency temporal modulations of speech and thus significantly improves intelligibility.

claims.claim: Real-time modulation enhancement (RMOD, ~2ms delay) raises objective intelligibility scores (extSII, Glimpse Proportion) above unmodified speech and improves combined intelligibility-quality scores (DWGP, STOI) to equal or better than original, while a strong file-based competitor (SSDRC) degrades quality scores below original.
claims.quote: the extSII and GP models report that the intelligibility score of RMOD is higher than that of the original speech... the DWGP and STOI that account for both for intelligibility and quality, score the RMOD higher or the same as original speech while SSDRC scores lower than original speech in the majority of the cases.

claims.claim: Modulation/clarity-enhancement DSP gains measured with real-world noise maskers (competing speaker, canteen noise, and their mix at SNRs from +11 to -3 dB) are quite low, contrasting with larger gains previously reported on artificial speech-shaped noise (SSN) — i.e., headline DSP intelligibility benefits shrink under realistic conditions.
claims.quote: Results suggest that the intelligibility gains for both algorithms are quite low when tested with real noises. This contrasts with previous results obtained for SSDRC on SSN and demonstrates the importance of testing speech enhancement algorithms in wide range of realistic conditions.

claims.claim: Modulation enhancement can be made real-time and low-latency (approx 2 ms delay) without requiring whole-file/look-ahead processing, by applying the compression separately in frequency and time domains, making it suitable for on-device/streaming pipelines.
claims.quote: RMOD is inspired by the DMOD [12], however it performs real-time energy reallocation (approx 2ms delay) and uses novel scaling operations to maintain speech quality.

claims.claim: Unlike aggressive static-curve compression (SSDRC), the modulation-enhancement approach preserves perceived speech quality: in a 22-listener preference test in quiet, RMOD scored similarly to unmodified speech while SSDRC received negative quality scores, and RMOD maintains long-term RMS energy independently of compression amount.
claims.quote: RMOD and unmodified speech appear to have similar preference scores, whereas SSDRC gives negative quality scores. This demonstrates that RMOD preserves the quality of speech.

claims.claim: STMI assesses speech intelligibility by using an auditory cortical model to analyze how distortions (noise, reverberation, etc.) affect the JOINT spectro-temporal modulations in speech, rather than the temporal-only modulations used by older indices like STI. This is directly relevant to the research question's angle on objective intelligibility metrics (STMI / spectro-temporal modulation index).
claims.quote: The method employs an auditory model to analyze the effects of noise, reverberations, and other distortions on the joint spectro-temporal modulations present in speech, and on the ability of a channel to transmit these modulations. The effects are summarized by a spectro-temporal modulation index (STMI).

claims.claim: STMI decomposes clean and degraded speech spectrograms into a 4-D spectro-temporal modulation representation using a biologically inspired modulation (Gabor) filter-bank — i.e., it measures intelligibility in a 2-D modulation domain (modulation along both time and frequency), which is the technical basis for using modulation-spectrum analysis to judge whether sped-up/processed speech remains comprehensible.
claims.quote: STMI ... uses a biologically inspired modulation Gabor filter-bank to decompose the clean and degraded speech spectrograms into a 4-D spectro-temporal modulation representation ... It analyzes the effects of distortions on the joint spectro-temporal modulations (in contrast to commonly used temporal-only modulation analysis) in speech.

claims.claim: STMI can predict speech intelligibility under degradations where the classical Speech Transmission Index (STI) fails, specifically phase jitter and frequency-dependent phase shifts — establishing a concrete advantage of spectro-temporal modulation analysis over temporal-only modulation indices for the kinds of spectral distortions introduced by TTS and DSP processing.
claims.quote: STMI is capable of predicting the speech intelligibility in the presence of several degradations where traditional SIP methods (such as STI) fail, e.g., phase jitter and frequency-dependent phase shifts.

claims.claim: STMI has documented limitations as an intelligibility predictor: it assumes a uniform contribution of different modulation frequencies and fails to accurately predict intelligibility for non-linear noise-reduction processing and for speech degraded by temporally modulated noise — a caveat to weigh before relying on STMI alone to decide whether a TTS fine-tune cleared a 'significant gain' bar.
claims.quote: STMI presumes a uniform contribution of different modulation frequencies to speech intelligibility ... it is not capable of accurately predicting intelligibility in some degradation conditions, including speech processed by non-linear noise reduction algorithms, and speech degraded by temporally modulated noise.

claims.claim: The STMI method was introduced by Mounya Elhilali, Taishih Chi, and Shihab A. Shamma in Speech Communication, Volume 41, Issues 2-3, pages 331-348, published October 2003; it is a highly cited foundational reference (200+ citations) for spectro-temporal intelligibility assessment.
claims.quote: A spectro-temporal modulation index (STMI) for assessment of speech intelligibility ... Speech Communication ... Volume 41, Issues 2-3, Pages 331-348 ... Mounya Elhilali, Taishih Chi, and Shihab A Shamma.

result.evidence: Quote verified verbatim in the abstract of arXiv:1901.07239 ("Non linear time compression of clear and normal speech at high rates," Valentini-Botinhao, Wester, Yamagishi et al., 2019, Edinburgh CSTR — established TTS/intelligibility researchers): "unprocessed fast speech was less intelligible than linearly compressed normal speech." The claim accurately restates this. I searched specifically for contradicting evidence and instead found strong independent corroboration: the Max Planck Institute study (Janse, "Word perception in fast speech: artificially time-compressed vs. naturally produced fast speech," Speech Communication / ScienceDirect) finds listeners PREFERRED artificially time-compressed speech, and that natural fast speech's "less careful articulation, combined with the changed timing pattern, slows down processing relative to linearly time-compressed speech." This is a replicated, multi-lab perceptual finding, not a fast-moving ML benchmark, so not outdated. Both sources are peer-reviewed academic work, not marketing/cherry-picked — source quality matches claim strength. One minor caveat (does not refute): the underlying studies compare HUMAN natural-fast vs. time-compressed HUMAN normal speech, not neural-TTS native-fast generation vs. TTS time-compression; the application to angle 3 (TTS pipeline) is an analogical extension. But the claim is appropriately hedged ("suggesting," "relevant to") and the core empirical assertion is correct and well-supported.
result.counterSource: No credible contradicting source found; search returned only corroborating evidence (Max Planck Institute / Janse, Speech Communication). Only nuance: studies test human speech, not TTS, so direct TTS generalization is inferential rather than measured.

result.evidence: The claim is well-supported by the primary source: Ferguson & Quené (2014), "Acoustic correlates of vowel intelligibility in clear and conversational speech," PMC4048446 (J. Acoust. Soc. Am.). The paper reports exactly the quoted finding: linear formant movement between nucleus endpoints (VL, vector length in F1xF2 space connecting 20% and 80% formant values) had significantly positive slopes predicting intelligibility (beta = 0.576, p < 0.0001), while non-linear movement within the nucleus (TLadj, the deviation of the trajectory from the straight-line vector VL) was unrelated to intelligibility (beta = -0.032, n.s.). The authors conclude "only linear movement was helpful for intelligibility." The claim accurately reports both the direction and the dissociation. The interpretive gloss ("movement carries vowel identity, not just static targets") is a reasonable, mainstream inference, independently corroborated by the well-established Vowel-Inherent Spectral Change (VISC) literature: vowels are more intelligible when formant trajectories are spectrally dynamic vs. flat, and models including spectral change outperform static-only models in vowel classification and correlation with human responses. No credible source contradicts this. The source is a peer-reviewed JASA paper appropriate for the claim's strength. Minor caveat: the claim's phrase "formant transitions" slightly conflates within-vowel inherent change (what the paper measures via VL/TLadj) with consonant-vowel transitions, but this does not refute the core empirical assertion.

The claim is well-supported by the primary source and corroborated independently. The source is arXiv 1901.07239, "Non linear time compression of clear and normal speech at high rates" by Valentini-Botinhao, Wester, Yamagishi, Toman, Pucher & Schabus (Centre for Speech Technology Research, Univ. of Edinburgh — established speech/TTS researchers; not marketing or a forum). WebFetch of the abstract and multiple web searches confirm the supporting quote verbatim: "compressed normal speech is more intelligible than compressed clear speech even when the overall duration of speech and silence is kept the same across styles." This directly supports both the core claim (normal > clear under aggressive time-compression) and the duration-controlled framing. The "inversion" framing is also corroborated: a search result states "Unprocessed fast speech was less intelligible than linearly compressed clear speech at moderate rates, but at the highest speaking rates clear speech was worse than fast speech" — i.e., the advantage holds at moderate rates and inverts only at the highest compression rates. IMPORTANT NUANCE (not a refutation): Krause & Braida (2002, JASA, PubMed 12430828) find that NATURALLY PRODUCED clear speech retains its advantage even at faster natural speaking rates ("clear speech has inherent acoustic properties that contribute to high intelligibility without altering rate"). This is a different mechanism (natural fast production) from signal-domain time-compression of pre-recorded slow clear speech, which is exactly the claim's scope, so it delineates rather than contradicts the claim. The finding is a stable psychoacoustic result, not a fast-moving benchmark, so the 2019 date is not a staleness concern. Two practical caveats on STRENGTH: (a) the inversion is specific to AGGRESSIVE/high compression rates, not all fast rates; (b) this is one study, so "can even invert" is appropriately hedged. The claim as worded ("does not survive aggressive time-compression and CAN even invert") matches the evidence's strength.
result.counterSource: Krause & Braida (2002), JASA 112(5):2165, PubMed 12430828 — finds naturally-produced clear speech retains its advantage at faster natural rates, but this concerns natural production not signal-domain time-compression, so it qualifies rather than refutes the claim's scope.

result.evidence: The claim is directly and verbatim supported by the primary source. arXiv:1901.07239 (a preprint of work in Speech Communication on time-compressed speech) states in its abstract: "unprocessed fast speech was less intelligible than linearly compressed normal speech." This is exactly the claim. The finding is corroborated by multiple independent peer-reviewed studies: (a) Janse et al., "Word perception in fast speech: artificially time-compressed vs. naturally produced fast speech" (Speech Communication, ScienceDirect S0167639303001092) found natural fast speech is less intelligible, partly due to reduced vowel space and uneven (non-linear) segment shortening; (b) the 2016 Springer perception study (10.3758/s13414-016-1067-x) found natural fast speech is perceived as faster and harder to process than linearly compressed speech; transfer of perceptual adaptation only went from compressed to natural, not vice versa. CRUCIALLY for the TTS/synthetic application domain, the study "Intelligibility of time-compressed synthetic speech: Compression method and speaking style" (Speech Communication, ScienceDirect S0167639315000977) found that "generating speech at a normal speaking rate and then applying linear compression resulted in the most intelligible speech at all tested rates" for English voices — directly extending the claim to synthetic/TTS speech and supporting the time-compression-beats-native-fast conclusion. No contradicting credible source was found. QUALIFICATIONS (do not refute, but bound the claim): (1) The arXiv finding concerns NATURAL human speech, not neural-TTS native-fast generation; the claim correctly hedges ("relevant to," "suggesting") rather than over-asserting TTS evidence. (2) The synthetic study adds a talker-dependent nuance: a skilled German speaker who articulated cleanly at fast rate (few deletions/substitutions) yielded intelligible results when his fast data was compressed, meaning the advantage of "normal-then-compress" is not absolute but depends on how cleanly the fast version is articulated. Source quality (peer-reviewed Speech Communication / Springer), recency-stability (psychoacoustics, not a fast-moving benchmark), and convergent multi-study support all match the claim's strength. Sources: https://arxiv.org/abs/1901.07239 ; https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0167639315000977 ; https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0167639303001092 ; https://link.springer.com/article/10.3758/s13414-016-1067-x

The claim is accurately supported, not an overreach. The primary source (Valentini-Botinhao, Wester, Yamagishi, Pucher et al., "Non-linear time compression of clear and normal speech at high rates," arXiv:1901.07239 / Univ. of Edinburgh, 2019) states in its abstract: "We found, in line with the literature, that unprocessed fast speech was less intelligible than linearly compressed normal speech." The supporting quote matches verbatim. CORROBORATION (independent, peer-reviewed): The foundational study Janse, E. (2004), "Word perception in fast speech: Artificially time-compressed vs. naturally produced fast speech," Speech Communication 42, 155-173 (Max Planck Institute) found that at 1.5x rate, naturally produced fast words were less intelligible than linearly compressed words and required more processing time, and that listeners PREFERRED artificially compressed speech, which had "both a temporal and a segmental advantage over natural fast speech" — because natural fast speech has "less careful articulation" (slurring) plus altered timing. This is replicated across multiple sources (ScienceDirect S0167639303001092; Springer s13414-016-1067-x). SOURCE QUALITY: peer-reviewed journal + reputable academic preprint, appropriate for the claim's strength; the phrase "in line with the literature" signals this is an established, multi-study finding, not a single-paper artifact. NOT OUTDATED: speech-rate perception is a stable psychoacoustic field, not fast-moving; findings span 2004-2019. NOT MARKETING. ONE NUANCE (refines, does not refute): natural speakers selectively preserve consonants/stressed syllables while compressing vowels, whereas LINEAR compression degrades all uniformly — this motivates NON-LINEAR compression (the arxiv paper's actual contribution) but does NOT overturn the specific claim that LINEAR time-compression beats natural fast speech. The claim is correctly scoped to linear compression and is well-supported.

The primary source PMC4048446 (Neel et al., "Acoustic correlates of vowel intelligibility in clear and conversational speech for young normal-hearing and elderly hearing-impaired listeners," peer-reviewed, PubMed 24907820, 2014) directly and verbatim supports the claim. WebFetch confirmed the GLMM results: VL (linear formant movement between 20%-80% nucleus endpoints in F1xF2 space) beta=0.576, p<0.0001 (highly significant positive effect on intelligibility); TLadj (non-linear deviation of the trajectory from the straight-line vector VL) beta=-0.032, n.s. (unrelated to intelligibility). Study sample: 41 talkers, 7 young normal-hearing + 40 elderly hearing-impaired listeners, 10 English vowels in /bVd/ context, clear vs conversational speech. The finding held for BOTH listener groups (VL effect slightly stronger for young NH, marginal group interaction p=0.0684 -- a nuance, not a contradiction). The interpretive extension ('preserving formant transitions/movement carries vowel identity, not just static targets') is independently corroborated by the broader Vowel Inherent Spectral Change (VISC) literature (Springer 'Vowel Inherent Spectral Change' 2013; JASA studies on VISC): dynamic-change models classify vowel categories far more accurately than steady-state models, and flat-formant (static) vowels show large identification-accuracy drops. No contradicting source found in two targeted searches. Source quality (peer-reviewed JASA-tier primary study with proper mixed-effects analysis) matches the claim's strength; the topic is foundational acoustic phonetics, not a fast-moving field, so 2014 publication date does not make it outdated.</parameter>

CLAIM SUPPORTED. Primary source verified by reading the full PDF (arXiv 1901.07239, Valentini-Botinhao, Wester, Yamagishi et al., CSTR Edinburgh / NII Japan — a peer-reviewed Speech-Synthesis-Workshop/Interspeech-class paper, logged in Edinburgh Research Explorer; not marketing/forum). The verbatim abstract and conclusions state: "even when the overall duration of speech and silence is kept the same across styles, compressed normal speech is still more intelligible than compressed clear speech." This duration-controlled result is the paper's central second-experiment finding, so the supporting quote is an accurate paraphrase, not an overreach. The inversion is also explicit: "at higher rates, compressed fast speech was more intelligible than compressed clear speech even though fast speech at its own rate was found to be less intelligible," and at 3x the clear-h advantage was lost (clear_h 43.6% word errors vs fast 28%). INDEPENDENT CORROBORATION: PMC2806443 review and the paper's cited refs [1][2] report "none of the time-scaling procedures produced speech that was more intelligible than unprocessed conversational speech." Mechanism is sound: time-compression degrades temporal-envelope and formant-transition cues that carry clear speech's gains, while steady-state vowel formants survive. CAVEATS (qualify but do not refute): (a) The result is established at AGGRESSIVE rates (2x-3x normal) — exactly the speed-listening regime in scope, so it is on-point rather than out-of-scope. (b) Genuine counter-evidence exists and is cited IN the paper: Krause & Braida [5] and ref [22] found clear speech produced/compressed at fast rates can retain a benefit over conversational speech — i.e., the literature is not unanimous; survival depends on whether clarity comes from duration (doesn't survive) vs inherent articulation/spectral cues (can survive). The reviewed claim is correctly scoped to time-compressing recorded clear-speech corpora at high rates, which is precisely where the advantage fails to survive. 2019 vintage is acceptable because the underlying psychoacoustics is stable, not a fast-moving benchmark. Source quality matches claim strength; current; well-supported.
result.counterSource: Krause & Braida (cited as ref [5] in arXiv 1901.07239) and ref [22] found clear speech compressed to fast/casual rates CAN remain more intelligible than conversational speech — a partial counterpoint showing the inversion is not universal and depends on the source of clarity (duration-based clarity does not survive compression; articulation/spectral-based clarity can). PMC2806443 corroborates the main claim.

result.evidence: VERIFIED against the primary source (Ferguson & Quené, "Acoustic correlates of vowel intelligibility in clear and conversational speech for young normal-hearing and elderly hearing-impaired listeners," J. Acoust. Soc. Am. 135(6):3570-3584, 2014 — peer-reviewed, PMC4048446). Every element of the claim matches the paper verbatim: (1) Listener groups = young normal-hearing (YNH, 19-30) vs elderly hearing-impaired (EHI, 65-87), matching "both groups... especially elderly hearing-impaired." (2) Mechanism: paper states "Longer vowel duration helped both groups but especially EHI listeners, perhaps by allowing more processing time for vowel identifications" — exact match including "more processing time." (3) Numbers: paper reports "average vowel durations of 290 ms in clear speech and 204 ms in conversational speech" and "vowel duration increased in clear speech by a factor of 1.5" — exact match to "~1.5 (290 ms vs 204 ms)." Source quality (JASA, top-tier acoustics journal) exceeds the bar for this claim. Not outdated: vowel lengthening in clear speech is a foundational, decades-replicated finding in speech acoustics (cf. Picheny et al. 1986), not a fast-moving field. Not marketing/cherry-picked — empirical study. One minor unstated nuance: the paper notes the per-vowel duration effect on identification odds was significant "only for tense vowels," which qualifies but does not contradict the directional claim (and the claim's own "perhaps"/"by giving more processing time" mirrors the paper's hedged phrasing).

CLAIM CONFIRMED, NOT REFUTED. Primary source verified by reading the full PDF of Valentini-Botinhao, Wester, Yamagishi, Toman, Pucher, Schabus, "Non linear time compression of clear and normal speech at high rates" (arXiv:1901.07239, Jan 2019; CSTR Edinburgh / NII Japan / FTW Vienna).

(1) Quote accuracy: The supporting quote is a faithful paraphrase of the abstract, which states verbatim: "We found that even when the overall duration of speech and silence is kept the same across styles, compressed normal speech is still more intelligible than compressed clear speech." The abstract also states the inversion explicitly: "Fast speech was also less intelligible than compressed clear speech but at the highest rate (three times faster than normal) the advantage of clear over fast speech was lost."

(2) Quantitative backing (Fig 3 / Sec 4.3, listening test with 20 native English normal-hearing listeners, word-error-rate): at 3x rate, linearly compressed CLEAR speech = 39.4% WER vs linearly compressed NORMAL speech = 20.3% WER. Even with duration of BOTH speech and silence equalized across styles (conditions N-NL3 vs C-NL3): normal = 15.3% WER vs clear = 28.2% WER. So normal is ~2x more intelligible — strong, not marginal, supporting both "does not survive" and "can invert."

(3) Independent corroboration: PMC2806443 (Krause & Braida lineage, JASA-affiliated) on time-scaling clear speech states "None of the time-scaling procedures produced speech that was more intelligible than unprocessed conversational speech," and "no condition where compressed clear speech exceeded conversational speech intelligibility at matched rates." Two independent research lines converge.

(4) Source quality matches claim strength: peer-reviewed-track authors who are leaders in TTS (Yamagishi, Valentini-Botinhao), controlled subjective listening tests, not marketing/forum. Finding is stable acoustic-phonetics (2002-2019 literature), not a fast-moving area, so not outdated.

LEGITIMATE SCOPE CAVEATS (do not refute, narrow applicability): (a) The clear speech tested was READ/acted clear speech; authors themselves note (Sec 5) it "may be" less robust than spontaneous communicative clear speech. (b) The finding concerns POST-HOC time-compression of recorded clear speech, not a TTS model trained to natively generate fast-but-clear speech — but the claim correctly limits its inference to "whether training on clear-speech corpora is worthwhile," which is exactly the supported conclusion.
result.counterSource: No credible counter-source found. Searched for contradicting evidence; the closest independent study (PMC2806443, Krause/Braida-lineage) AGREES that time-scaled clear speech never beats conversational speech at matched rate. The only related "clear/normal at normal rate retains 78% of benefit" finding concerns naturally-produced clear-normal speech, NOT time-compression, so it does not contradict the claim.

result.evidence: The claim is well-supported by the primary source (PMC4048446, "Acoustic correlates of vowel intelligibility in clear and conversational speech for young normal-hearing and elderly hearing-impaired listeners"). I fetched the article directly and verified every number: (1) F2 range — paper reports "clear/conversational ratio for F2 range was 1.14 on average [95% C.I. (1.09,1.19)]," i.e. ~14%, with "an average F2 range increase of 0.38 Bark" — exactly matches the claim. (2) F1 range — paper reports "clear/conversational ratio for F1 range was 1.09 on average [95% C.I. (1.05, 1.13)]," i.e. ~9%, with a 0.23 Bark increase — matches the claim. (Note: the paper's prose has an internal typo writing "increase of the F2 range by 0.23 Bark" in the F1 sentence, but the 1.09 ratio and 0.23 Bark are unambiguously the F1-range figures; the claim correctly attributes 0.23 Bark / 9% to F1.) (3) Directionality — paper states "the front vowels /i/, /ɪ/, /e/, /ε/, and /æ/ showed significantly higher F2 values in clear speech, while the back vowels /o/, /ʊ/, and /u/ had significantly lower F2 values" — exactly matches "front-vowel F2 increases and back-vowel F2 decreases." Independent corroboration: WebSearch of the clear-speech literature (Maniwa et al., Brunel hyperarticulation thesis, Springer/PMC hyperarticulation studies) independently confirms the same well-established pattern — vowel-space expansion, F1 increasing across vowels, higher F2 for front vowels and lower F2 for low/back vowels. No credible source contradicts these figures; this is a stable, decades-old, peer-reviewed finding in speech acoustics (not a fast-moving field where age matters). One mild caveat the literature notes (only an associative, not yet proven causal, link between vowel-space expansion and intelligibility) does not affect this claim, which is descriptive/quantitative about the acoustic magnitudes, not a causal assertion. Source quality (peer-reviewed JASA/PMC primary article) matches the claim strength.

VERIFIED against the primary source (Ferguson & Quené 2014, "Acoustic correlates of vowel intelligibility in clear and conversational speech for young normal-hearing and elderly hearing-impaired listeners," JASA 135(6):3570-3584, PMC4048446). All three quantitative/qualitative components of the claim are directly and accurately supported: (1) The paper states verbatim "Longer vowel duration helped both groups but especially EHI listeners, perhaps by allowing more processing time for vowel identifications and/or by increasing the temporal contrast between spectrally similar tense-lax pairs." (2) The paper gives exact durations: "average vowel durations of 290 ms in clear speech and 204 ms in conversational speech." (3) The paper states "Averaged across all 41 talkers, vowel duration increased in clear speech by a factor of 1.5." Source quality is sufficient: this is a peer-reviewed primary study in the top acoustics journal (JASA), directly on the topic (clear speech, vowel duration, hearing-impaired listeners). MINOR CAVEAT (does not meet refutation bar): the paper HEDGES the mechanism ("perhaps by allowing more processing time...and/or by increasing temporal contrast"), offering two candidate explanations, whereas the claim asserts the single "processing time" mechanism more confidently and drops the hedge — but the substance is faithful. Broader literature (e.g. Ferguson 2012, PMC3370057) notes the OVERALL clear-speech advantage is weaker/absent for some EHI listeners, but that concerns the full clear-speech benefit, not the specific vowel-duration component, so it does not contradict this claim. Not outdated for foundational psychoacoustics (mechanism is stable knowledge), not marketing, not cherry-picked.

result.evidence: The primary source (Ferguson & Quené, PMC4048446, "Acoustic correlates of vowel intelligibility in clear and conversational speech for young normal-hearing and elderly hearing-impaired listeners") verbatim confirms every number in the claim. F2: "The clear/conversational ratio for F2 range was 1.14 on average [...] the 14% expansion corresponds with an average F2 range increase of 0.38 Bark]". F1: "ratio for F1 range was 1.09 on average [...] the 9% expansion corresponds with an average increase of the F1 range by 0.23 Bark]". Source quality is high (peer-reviewed JASA-class study, NIH PMC). Only nuance: the "aids intelligibility" framing is association, not universal causation — this paper found expansion improved vowel intelligibility for young normal-hearing listeners but NOT for elderly hearing-impaired listeners. result.evidence:
VERDICT: NOT REFUTED. The claim's two core empirical assertions are directly and accurately supported by the primary source, which is a top-tier peer-reviewed venue.

SOURCE VERIFIED: PMC4048446 = Ferguson & Quené (2014), "Acoustic correlates of vowel intelligibility in clear and conversational speech for young normal-hearing and elderly hearing-impaired listeners," J. Acoust. Soc. Am. 135(6):3570-3584. Confirmed via PubMed (24907820) and ResearchGate. This is JASA — an authoritative, peer-reviewed acoustics journal. Sample: 41 talkers, 7 young normal-hearing + 40 elderly hearing-impaired listeners, /bVd/ vowels in noise, generalized linear mixed modeling.

CHECK 1 (quote support): The supporting quote is accurate. The article verbatim: "greater linear formant movement between the endpoints of the vowel nucleus (VL) was associated with higher vowel intelligibility, while non-linear movement within the nucleus (TLadj) was unrelated to vowel intelligibility." VL = Euclidean vector length in F1×F2 space connecting the 20% and 80% time points of the vowel nucleus (β positive, significant); TLadj = curvature/non-linear deviation from that straight line (non-significant). Both core empirical claims match.

CHECK 2 (contradicting evidence): None found. The broader dynamic-specification literature CORROBORATES rather than contradicts (e.g., PMC4315365 "Dynamic Spectral Structure Specifies Vowels," ScienceDirect diphthong studies showing dynamic targets more intelligible than static, formant-transition splicing studies showing identification collapses when transitions are wrong). Consensus supports that formant movement carries vowel identity.

CHECK 3 (source quality): Primary, peer-reviewed, high-impact. Matches claim strength.

CHECK 4 (outdated): 2014, but this is foundational speech-perception psychoacoustics — a stable field, not fast-moving. Not outdated.

CHECK 5 (marketing/cherry-pick): No. Academic study, mixed-effects modeling, both listener groups showed the pattern.

ONE CAVEAT (does not rise to refutation): The claim's interpretive tail "not just static targets" slightly overstates. The SAME study found steady-state formants F1 and F2 ALSO significantly predicted intelligibility ("Identification accuracy is strongly correlated with F1 ... also correlated with F2"). So movement is NOT the sole carrier of vowel identity — static targets matter too. The claim should not imply static targets are unimportant. However, the operational recommendation embedded in the claim (preserve formant movement/transitions to maintain intelligibility, especially relevant under time-compression) is valid and supported. Also note a scope subtlety: VL measures movement WITHIN the nucleus (20%-80% points), not consonant-vowel transitions at syllable margins, so equating this specifically with "formant transitions" is loose terminology — but the dynamic-movement principle holds.

Net: Core claims accurate, source authoritative and primary, current, corroborated by independent literature. Minor framing over-emphasis ("not just static") is a quibble, not grounds for refutation.
result.counterSource: No credible contradicting source found. The nearest qualifier is that the same study (Ferguson & Quené 2014) also reports static formants F1/F2 as significant intelligibility predictors, which tempers the claim's "not just static targets" phrasing but does not refute the VL/TLadj findings.

result.evidence: Verified directly against the primary source (Ferguson & Quené 2014, "Acoustic correlates of vowel intelligibility in clear and conversational speech for young normal-hearing and elderly hearing-impaired listeners," J. Acoust. Soc. Am., PMC4048446). The paper states verbatim: F2 range clear/conversational ratio = 1.14 on average [95% CI (1.09,1.19), t(40)=5.6, p<0.0001; the 14% expansion corresponds to a 0.38 Bark increase]; and F1 range ratio = 1.09 [95% CI (1.05,1.13), t(40)=4.3, p=0.0001; the 9% expansion = 0.23 Bark]. The directional claim is also confirmed verbatim: front vowels /i,ɪ,e,ɛ,æ/ showed significantly HIGHER F2 in clear speech, back vowels /o,ʊ,u/ had significantly LOWER F2 — exactly matching the "front-vowel F2 increases, back-vowel F2 decreases" claim. WebSearch found NO contradicting evidence; corroborated by Ferguson & Kewley-Port (2002/2007), Maniwa et al. (2007), and a 2021 Springer hyperarticulation study. Source is peer-reviewed, statistically robust (n=41 talkers, significant p-values w/ CIs), current enough for stable phonetic science, and not marketing. The only flaw is a garbled/non-verbatim "supporting quote" string (stray quotation marks), but every number and direction it asserts is an exact match to the paper — a transcription artifact, not a substantive misread.

VERIFIED against the cited primary source (PMC4048446, a peer-reviewed J Acoust Soc Am study on clear/conversational vowels). (1) NUMBERS CONFIRMED VERBATIM: the paper states "average vowel durations of 290 ms in clear speech and 204 ms in conversational speech" = ~1.42x, which matches the claim's "~1.5 (290 ms vs 204 ms)." (2) SUPPORTING QUOTE IS VERBATIM ACCURATE: Discussion section literally reads "Longer vowel duration helped both groups but especially EHI listeners, perhaps by allowing more processing time for vowel identifications and/or by increasing the temporal contrast between spectrally similar tense-lax pairs." The two groups are YNH (young normal-hearing, 19-30) and EHI (elderly hearing-impaired, 65-87). (3) The "more processing time" mechanism and the "especially EHI" emphasis are the authors' own words. CAVEAT (does not refute): the Results/GLMM section is slightly more restrictive than the Discussion summary — the duration effect was statistically significant only for EHI listeners on tense vowels; for YNH the regression slopes of duration on intelligibility were "approximately equal (and zero)." So strictly, the model showed no significant duration benefit for YNH. However, this Discussion-vs-Results tension originates IN the source itself; the claim faithfully paraphrases the authors' own synthesis sentence and correctly hedges with "especially EHI." No external source contradicts the clear-speech vowel-lengthening finding (it is a well-replicated, stable psychoacoustic result, not a fast-moving field). Source quality matches claim strength: primary peer-reviewed paper, exact citation, numbers and quote both confirmed.

VERIFIED against primary source. Extracted the exact text from the Maniwa, Jongman & Wade (2007) Interspeech PDF (isca-archive.org). The verbatim sentence reads: "Clear speech elicited in laboratory settings has been shown to benefit a variety of listener populations under different adverse conditions (e.g. noise, reverberation) [e.g. 3, 4, 5], resulting in intelligibility advantages relative to 'conversational' speech ranging from 7 to 38 percentage points." This matches the claim's '7 to 38 percentage points' figure exactly. The SNR-threshold finding is independently confirmed by the peer-reviewed JASA publication of the same study (Maniwa, Jongman & Wade 2008, J. Acoust. Soc. Am. 123(2):1114-1125, PubMed 18247912): "Two experiments measured babble signal-to-noise ratio thresholds for fricative minimal pair distinctions for 14 normal-hearing listeners and 14 listeners with simulated sloping, recruiting impairment. Results indicated that clear speech helped both groups overall." The study is explicitly about fricative consonants. MINOR QUALIFICATION (not a refutation): the abstract notes "for impaired listeners, reliable clear speech intelligibility advantages were not found for non-sibilant pairs" — i.e., the benefit for impaired listeners was overall but not universal across all fricative pairs. The claim does not assert universality, so this is a nuance rather than a contradiction. Source quality (ISCA peer-reviewed conference + JASA journal) matches claim strength; age (2007/2008) is fine for foundational acoustic-phonetics science. Note: also independently observed that the SAME paper immediately adds a robustness caveat ("some results call into question the robustness of the 'clear speech' effect... negative clear-speech benefits in elderly hearing-impaired listeners"), but this does not undermine the specific claim under review.

The claim is a near-verbatim, accurate paraphrase of the primary source (Maniwa, Jongman & Wade, Interspeech 2007, "Acoustic correlates of intelligibility enhancements in clearly produced fricatives", DOI 10.21437/Interspeech.2007-199). I extracted the full PDF text. Experiment 1 (14 normal-hearing listeners, VCV fricative pairs in babble) reports verbatim: "Significant positive correlations (p<0.05) were obtained between intelligibility advantages and acoustic modifications in DFT and LPC peak location and spectral moment 1 (M1), whereas significant negative correlations were found for M3. These results suggest that a shift of spectral energy to higher frequency regions in clear fricatives is most closely related to the overall intelligibility enhancement." The abstract independently confirms: "a shift of energy concentration toward higher frequency regions and greater source strength contributed to the clear speech effect for normal-hearing listeners." (1) The claim is fully supported by the quote, not an overreach; "dominant" maps faithfully to the paper's "most closely related." (2) No contradicting evidence found; the peer-reviewed JASA 2008 journal version of the same study (J Acoust Soc Am 123(2):1114, PMID 18247912) restates the identical conclusion, and later work (JASA Express Letters 2025) treats M1/spectral-peak as standard place-of-articulation correlates without disputing the clear-speech direction. (3) Source quality is strong (ISCA peer-reviewed + corroborating JASA journal article by established phoneticians) and matches the claim's strength. (4) Not outdated — this is stable phonetics/psychoacoustics. (5) Not marketing or cherry-picked. The claim correctly restricts the finding to NORMAL-HEARING listeners; this is important because the paper found the OPPOSITE direction for simulated hearing-impaired listeners (higher-frequency shifts hurt them, with sibilant benefit coming from lower-frequency cues). Two minor, non-refuting nuances: the paper also credits "greater source strength" alongside the spectral shift, and the clear-speech effect was absent/reversed for the non-sibilant /f/-/θ/ contrast under hearing-loss simulation — but neither weakens the claim as stated, which is scoped to normal-hearing listeners.
result.counterSource: No credible contradicting source found. The peer-reviewed JASA 2008 journal version (J Acoust Soc Am 123(2):1114, PMID 18247912) and 2025 JASA Express Letters work on spectral moments both corroborate rather than refute the M1/spectral-peak-to-intelligibility link for normal-hearing listeners.

result.evidence: Verified against full extracted PDF text (Maniwa, Jongman & Wade, "Acoustic correlates of intelligibility enhancements in clearly produced fricatives," Interspeech 2007, DOI 10.21437/Interspeech.2007-199). It is in the introduction as a summary of prior references [3,4,5], and the claim correctly attributes it to "prior literature" — no misread. (2) SNR-threshold finding is supported: Exp 1 normal-hearing listeners showed a significant main effect of Style (p<.0001) with lower clear-speech SNR thresholds; Exp 2 with simulated sloping/recruiting hearing loss showed a main effect of Style (p<.001) "indicated, again, an overall clear speech advantage across listener groups" with no Style×Group interaction, i.e. both groups benefited similarly — exactly as claimed. (3) The study is entirely about consonants/fricatives (8 fricatives, VCV minimal-pair identification in babble), matching "for consonants/fricatives." Minor omitted nuance (does not contradict the headline): for the simulated-impaired group the benefit was robust mainly for sibilants, with a small non-significant ANTI-clear-speech effect for non-sibilant /f/-/θ/; but the abstract itself states "Clear speech benefited both groups overall," so the claim's "both" is faithful. Independent web search corroborates the magnitude (Picheny 1985 ~17pp average; documented improvements up to ~26pp; benefits of ~14-18pp across studies) — consistent with, not contradicting, a 7-38pp envelope across conditions/populations. No contradicting credible source found; not marketing/press-release/forum; 2007 date is fine for well-established clear-speech acoustic-phonetics. Claim is supported, current, and well-sourced.
