This is corroborated by multiple INDEPENDENT peer-reviewed studies, not a single source: Janse (2003), "Word perception in fast speech: artificially time-compressed vs. naturally produced fast speech" (Speech Communication/ScienceDirect) found natural fast speech is harder/slower to process than time-compressed; Springer (2016) "Natural fast speech is perceived as faster than linearly time-compressed speech"; and review literature stating "linear time compression preserves intelligibility better than natural fast speech" and that listeners "preferred artificially time-compressed speech over naturally produced fast speech." No contradicting source was found. The finding is a stable psychoacoustic result spanning 2003–2016+, not a fast-moving field, so not outdated, and it is academic (not marketing). The claim's quote is matched without overreach. Note: the directional implication is actually favorable to the user's TC pipeline (TC is MORE intelligible than genuinely fast natural speech at the same rate), but this does not refute the claim — it is precisely what the claim asserts ("a TC voice does not behave like genuinely fast speech").

The claim is well-supported by the primary source (PMC6377055, Gordon-Salant et al.) and corroborated by independent peer-reviewed literature. The source explicitly states: "each pairwise comparison among the three rates in both contexts was significant (p <= 0.006) except for HP normal rate vs 40TC" and that "performance was best for the normal rate speech, significantly poorer for the 40TC rate, and poorest with natural fast speech." Critically, the rate-matching is valid: the natural fast speech and 40TC speech were duration-matched (both ~0.6 fast-to-normal ratio); the talker's natural fast sentences "approximated 40% time-compression (fast-to-normal ratio = 0.6)," so 40% was chosen for the TC condition to match. Thus the comparison is genuinely at matched rate, supporting the claim that uniform time-compression is more intelligible than naturally fast speech. This is NOT an isolated result: it converges with Janse (2003, "Word perception in fast speech: artificially time-compressed vs. naturally produced fast speech," Speech Communication, Max Planck Institute), which found "at 1.5 times normal rate, naturally produced fast words were less intelligible than linearly compressed words" and that "linearly time-compressed speech has both a temporal and a segmental advantage over natural fast speech," with listeners preferring time-compressed speech. The mechanism is established: natural fast speech compresses segments non-uniformly (vowels shorten more, unstressed syllables deleted), reducing articulatory precision. Two minor caveats that do not refute the claim: (1) the p <= 0.006 figure is specifically the "in quiet" pairwise comparisons (in noise the rate effect was p < 0.001), and one comparison (HP listeners, normal vs 40TC) was not significant -- but the 40TC-vs-natural-fast and normal-vs-natural-fast comparisons underpinning the stated ranking were significant; (2) tested population was normal-hearing younger (19-22) and older (66-76) adults repeating sentences, not blind power-listeners at 18-22 syl/sec, so generalization to extreme rates is an extrapolation. Source quality is high (peer-reviewed journal, primary study) and the field finding is stable/not outdated.

The claim is well-supported by the primary source AND corroborated by independent literature. PMC6377055 (Gordon-Salant et al., normal-hearing adults, 13 younger ages 19-22 and 12 older ages 66-76) explicitly states: "Performance was best for the normal rate speech, significantly poorer for the 40TC rate, and poorest with natural fast speech" with p ≤ 0.006 for pairwise comparisons in quiet. The 40TC (40% time-compression, fast-to-normal ratio 0.6) was DELIBERATELY chosen to match the duration of the natural fast recordings (which averaged ~40% compression), so the comparison IS at a matched rate — directly validating the claim's "at matched rate" framing. The ranking held in both quiet and noise. The study's own conclusion: "Recognition of time-compressed sentences over-estimated recognition of natural fast sentences for both groups, especially for older listeners." Independent corroboration: Janse (2003/2004, Max Planck / Speech Communication) found "linearly time-compressed speech has both a temporal and a segmental advantage over natural fast speech," that listeners "preferred artificially time-compressed speech," and that natural fast speech is harder due to slurred/unequal-segment articulation (vowels shorten more than consonants, unstressed syllables deleted). The generalization that uniform time-compression beats natural fast speech at matched rate is thus supported by multiple converging peer-reviewed sources, not a single study. Minor caveat: the p≤0.006 in the source applies to quiet pairwise comparisons; the claim's parenthetical is accurate. Caveats not in the claim: study was normal-hearing only (not power-listener/screen-reader population), and the rate (~40% compression of conversational speech) is far below the 18-22 syllables/sec extreme-speed regime of the research question — so the finding supports the principle but does not directly speak to the extreme rates relevant to the pipeline.

All three sub-claims are directly verified. (1) Segmental distortion: PMC6377055 states verbatim that natural fast recordings show "not only shorter overall phoneme... durations, but also deletions and distortions, especially of stops and affricates," caused by "co-articulation... and lenition, in which there is undershoot in reaching the target phoneme." The quote is faithfully represented, not overreached. (2) TC preservation: the same paper states PSOLA-type algorithms "preserve the temporal fine structure pattern and spectral information in speech, but in a reduced time scale." (3) The implied intelligibility consequence is also supported by the paper itself ("significantly poorer recognition scores for natural fast-rate speech than for TC speech... in quiet and noise, for both high- and low-context sentences") AND independently corroborated by separate peer-reviewed work: Janse et al. (Speech Communication / ScienceDirect, Max Planck Institute) and related Adank/Janse studies, which find "linearly time-compressed speech has both a temporal and a segmental advantage over natural fast speech" because natural fast speech's "less careful/slurred articulation" slows processing. No credible source contradicts the claim. The only qualifier found (arXiv 1901.07239; biorxiv ECoG work) is that TC has absolute intelligibility limits at ultra-fast rates (>~3x), where temporal locking to the envelope collapses — but this does not say natural fast speech beats TC; it concerns TC's own ceiling, leaving the comparative claim intact. Sources are peer-reviewed primary research indexed by NIH/ScienceDirect/Springer; this is settled speech-perception science, so the 2003-2019 publication dates are not a recency problem. No marketing, forum speculation, or cherry-picked benchmark involved.
result.counterSource: Closest qualifier (not a refutation): arXiv 1901.07239 "Non linear time compression of clear and normal speech at high rates" and biorxiv ECoG study (10.1101/354464) note TC has absolute intelligibility limits at ultra-fast rates where neural envelope-locking breaks down; this bounds TC's ceiling but does not reverse the TC-vs-natural-fast comparison.

result.evidence: VERIFIED against primary source PMC6377055. (1) RANKING CONFIRMED: The paper states verbatim "performance was best for the normal rate speech, significantly poorer for the 40TC rate, and poorest with natural fast speech" — exactly the normal > 40TC > natural-fast ordering claimed. (2) P-VALUE CONFIRMED: "Post hoc analysis of the context × rate interaction revealed that each pairwise comparison among the three rates in both contexts was significant (p ≤ 0.006)" — the claim's "p ≤ 0.006" is a direct quote match, and because ALL pairwise comparisons were significant, the 40TC-vs-natural-fast pair is included. (3) MATCHED RATE CONFIRMED: natural fast speech averaged ~40% time-compression (fast-to-normal ratio = 0.6), and 40TC was selected specifically to match that rate — so "at matched rate" is accurate, not an overreach. (4) SOURCE QUALITY ADEQUATE: peer-reviewed study on normal-hearing adults (younger 19-22, n=13; older 66-76, n=12); 40TC produced via PSOLA preserving fine structure, natural fast speech showed deletions/distortions of stops/affricates. (5) NO CONTRADICTING EVIDENCE — INDEPENDENTLY REPLICATED: WebSearch surfaced multiple converging peer-reviewed studies (Janse et al., Speech Communication 2004 "Word perception in fast speech: artificially time-compressed vs. naturally produced fast speech"; Springer 2016 "Natural fast speech is perceived as faster than linearly time-compressed speech") all finding linearly time-compressed speech is MORE intelligible than natural fast speech at matched rate, because natural fast speech compresses segments unequally (vowels shorten more than consonants, unstressed syllables deleted). REFUTATION ATTEMPTS FAILED on all fronts: quote support, p-value, rate-matching, source quality, and external corroboration all hold. Minor non-refuting caveat: the framing "establishing that..." is slightly strong for a single study, but the generalization is justified by independent replication.

The claim is well-supported and NOT refuted. The primary source PMC6377055 (Gordon-Salant et al., "Recognition of time-compressed speech does not predict recognition of natural fast-rate speech by older listeners") directly states that acoustic analyses of natural fast recordings showed "not only shorter overall phoneme durations, but also deletions and distortions, especially of stops and affricates," attributing these to "co-articulation... and lenition, in which there is undershoot in reaching the target phoneme" — verbatim support for the claim's segmental-distortion mechanism. The same source confirms time-compression algorithms (PSOLA) "preserve the temporal fine structure pattern and spectral information in speech, but in a reduced time scale" with "minimal stimulus degradation," supporting the claim's second half. The intelligibility direction is also confirmed: recognition scores were "considerably lower for naturally fast-rate speech than for either 40TC speech or normal-rate speech." This is INDEPENDENTLY CORROBORATED by multiple peer-reviewed studies: Janse (2003/2004, ScienceDirect S0167639303001092) "Word perception in fast speech: artificially time-compressed vs. naturally produced fast speech" found "unprocessed fast speech was less intelligible than linearly compressed normal speech" and that "linearly compressed speech... preferred over a nonlinearly compressed version... where fast speech prosodic patterns were mimicked," and Adank & Janse (Springer s13414-016-1067-x). No credible contradicting source was found; the body of evidence is convergent. Caveat: the primary study's headline finding concerns older listeners and the non-predictive relationship between TC and natural-fast recognition, but the specific acoustic mechanism in the claim is a well-supported, independently replicated finding, not an overreach of the quote. Source quality (multiple peer-reviewed journals: PMC/NIH, ScienceDirect/Speech Communication, Springer) matches the claim strength.

CLAIM CONFIRMED — could not refute across all checklist items. (1) Quote support: PMC6377055 directly states natural fast recordings show "not only shorter overall phoneme durations, but also deletions and distortions, especially of stops and affricates," and (verified via fetch) attributes these to "co-articulation... and lenition, in which there is undershoot in reaching the target phoneme" — exactly the claim's mechanism. The paper also confirms the TC half: PSOLA-style methods "preserve the temporal fine structure pattern and spectral information in speech, but in a reduced time scale" with "minimal stimulus degradation, other than the shift in time scale." The paper's recognition data show "Recognition scores are considerably lower for naturally fast speech than for either 40TC speech or normal-rate speech," so the intelligibility direction is empirically grounded, not just mechanistic speculation. (2) Adversarial contra-search FAILED to refute: it instead produced independent corroboration — Janse et al. "Word perception in fast speech: artificially time-compressed vs. naturally produced fast speech" (Speech Communication / ScienceDirect, 2004, Max Planck) and Adank & Janse (Springer, Atten Percept Psychophys 2016) both independently find linearly time-compressed speech more intelligible than natural fast speech, citing "less careful articulation," "changed timing pattern," and "reduced vowel space." (3) Source quality matches claim strength: peer-reviewed primary acoustic+perception studies, not marketing/forum/benchmark. (4) Not outdated: underlying phonetics (co-articulation, lenition, undershoot) is settled science; corroborating literature spans 2004–2020. ONE NON-FATAL NUANCE: the absolute "TC does not introduce [any] distortion" framing is slightly strong — uniform TC adds its own time-scale degradation at extreme rates, and variable-rate (per-phoneme) compression can beat uniform TC (USPTO 7,065,485; consonant-preserving methods). But these refine rather than contradict the core comparison (natural fast < uniform TC because of segmental distortions absent from TC), which holds firmly.
result.counterSource: No credible refutation found. Searched-for contradicting evidence instead corroborated the claim: Janse et al., "Word perception in fast speech," Speech Communication 2004 (https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0167639303001092); Adank & Janse, Atten Percept Psychophys 2016 (https://link.springer.com/article/10.3758/s13414-016-1067-x). Only minor qualifier: variable-rate TSM can outperform uniform TC (USPTO 7,065,485), which does not contradict the natural-fast-vs-uniform-TC claim.

result.evidence:
The empirical half of the claim is well-sourced and accurate. The source is Janse, E. (2004), "Word perception in fast speech: Artificially time-compressed vs. naturally produced fast speech," Speech Communication 42:155-173 (DOI 10.1016/j.specom.2003.07.001), a peer-reviewed Max Planck Institute study; the supporting quote matches the abstract verbatim and is corroborated by related work. So it is TRUE that linear time-compression is processed faster and preferred over NATURALLY PRODUCED (human-reduced) fast speech, even when both are intelligible.

However, the claim's load-bearing inference — that this "directly undercuts the assumption that training a TTS to natively produce human-style fast speech would beat simply time-compressing a normal voice" — is an overreach not supported by the source and contradicted for the relevant use case: (1) Category error — Janse's "natural fast speech" is a human REDUCING articulation under time pressure (vowel deletion, undershoot, sloppy consonants); that is the opposite of a TTS fine-tuned on CLEAR-SPEECH/Lombard/hyperarticulated corpora, which is exactly what the research question proposes. The paper penalizes reduction, not native fast generation, and tests nothing about clear-speech synthesis. (2) The ultra-fast regime flips: at 2.5x-4.1x (the ~18-22 syl/s power-listener band the question targets), non-linear/content-aware compression (MACH1) BEATS linear; linear only dominates at moderate ~1.4x fast rates (Speech Communication 2015, S0167639315000977; Springer 10.3758/s13414-016-1067-x). So "linear always wins" is false precisely where it matters. (3) Compression only shrinks time — it cannot add vowel-space expansion or consonant emphasis that a clear-speech-trained model can synthesize natively. Thus the strong directive ("directly undercuts") is unsupported by Janse 2004 on its load-bearing portion and contradicted by the ultra-fast literature.
result.counterSource: https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0167639315000977 (Intelligibility of time-compressed synthetic speech: compression method and speaking style); https://link.springer.com/article/10.3758/s13414-016-1067-x (Natural fast speech is perceived as faster than linearly time-compressed speech); MACH1 non-linear compression beating linear at ultra-fast rates

result.evidence: CORROBORATED. The exact figures (572 / 600 / 624 ms) appear in Esther Janse's work on word perception in natural-fast vs. artificially time-compressed speech. An independent WebSearch over the ResearchGate paper "Word perception in natural-fast and artificially time-compressed speech" returned: "linear time-compression had a mean detection time of 572 ms, copy-fast-speech-timing had 600 ms, and natural fast speech had 624 ms, all with 3% miss rates." The 52 ms advantage (624−572) is internally consistent with the claim's "Only the 52 ms advantage of linear compression over natural fast speech is significant." Multiple independent searches confirm the directional finding that linearly time-compressed speech yields significantly faster phoneme-detection reaction times than natural fast speech (Janse 2003b/2004, replicated/cited by Adank & Janse 2009). SOURCE QUALITY: high — peer-reviewed primary source, Speech Communication (Elsevier; S0167639303 DOI prefix matches that journal) by Janse at the Max Planck Institute for Psycholinguistics, with a companion ICPhS 2003 (Barcelona) proceedings paper. Not marketing, not a forum claim, not outdated for a settled psycholinguistic finding. MINOR CAVEAT (non-refuting): the precise 572/600/624 triplet including the copy-fast-speech-timing condition is reported most explicitly in the ICPhS 2003 proceedings companion paper rather than verbatim in the cited journal article; both report the same experiments, so this is a citation-precision nuance, not a factual error. Direct fetch of the ScienceDirect/ResearchGate pages returned HTTP 403 (paywall/anti-bot), so verification rests on search-engine extraction of the paper's content plus multiple corroborating secondary citations — all consistent.

VERIFIED, NOT REFUTED. The supporting quote is verbatim from the abstract of Janse, E. (2004), "Word perception in fast speech: artificially time-compressed vs. naturally produced fast speech," Speech Communication 42:155-173 (Max Planck Institute for Psycholinguistics) — confirmed via the official MPI publication record (mpi.nl/publications/item66910). The abstract states verbatim: "Even when naturally produced fast speech is perfectly intelligible, its less careful articulation, combined with the changed timing pattern, slows down processing, relative to linearly time-compressed speech. Furthermore, listeners preferred artificially time-compressed speech over naturally produced fast speech. These results suggest that linearly time-compressed speech has both a temporal and a segmental advantage over natural fast speech." The claim's first sentence (processed faster AND preferred, even when both intelligible) is a faithful, non-overreaching summary.

(1) Source quality: peer-reviewed primary research in a leading journal; adequate for claim strength. (2) Not outdated: this is human speech-perception psychoacoustics (slow-moving, foundational), not a fast-moving ML benchmark; the mechanism (segmental reduction/slurring in natural fast speech impairs processing) is robust. (3) Contradiction search: the most plausible candidate, Reinisch (2016) "Natural fast speech is perceived as faster than linearly time-compressed speech" (Atten Percept Psychophys, PubMed 26860711), does NOT contradict — it addresses *perceived rate* (orthogonal dimension) and is consistent with natural fast speech having distinctive segmental properties. No credible source disputes Janse's processing/preference finding. Not marketing/cherry-picked.

ONE QUALIFICATION (does not falsify): The claim's second sentence ("undercuts training a TTS to natively produce human-style fast speech") is a sound but slightly broad inference. Janse compared natural *reductive* fast speech vs compressed *normal* speech — it did NOT test compressed *clear/hyperarticulated* speech. Krause & Braida (e.g., PubMed 14759028, 12430828) show clear/hyperarticulated speech has rate-INDEPENDENT intelligibility gains, so a TTS trained on clear speech then compressed could plausibly beat compressing normal speech. Thus the claim correctly undercuts mimicking human *reductive* fast speech, but does not rule out clear-speech conditioning. Since human fast speech is inherently reductive, "human-style fast speech" is the correct target of the critique, so the core claim stands.
result.counterSource: Reinisch (2016), "Natural fast speech is perceived as faster than linearly time-compressed speech," Attention Perception & Psychophysics (PubMed 26860711) — examined but does NOT contradict; concerns perceived rate, not processing/preference. Krause & Braida clear-speech work (PubMed 14759028, 12430828) provides only a minor qualification re: clear-speech conditioning, not a refutation.

result.evidence:
The core factual claim is well-supported by the cited primary source and independently corroborated. Source verified: Janse, E. (2004), "Word perception in fast speech: artificially time-compressed vs. naturally produced fast speech," Speech Communication 42(2):155-173 (ScienceDirect S0167639303001092) — a peer-reviewed primary source from a Max Planck Institute psycholinguist. The supporting quote matches the abstract. The finding (natural reduced fast speech is processed slower and less preferred than linearly time-compressed speech of the same rate) is independently confirmed by Valentini-Botinhao, Wester, Yamagishi et al. (2019, CSTR Edinburgh / NII Japan), arXiv:1901.07239: "Janse and colleagues have shown that fast speech (~1.56x) is harder to process, in terms of reaction times, and is less preferred than linearly compressed speech... linearly compressed normal speech was more intelligible than uncompressed fast speech," and even "compressed normal speech is still more intelligible than compressed clear speech" when speech+silence durations are equated. Janse's own follow-up (Word-level intelligibility of time-compressed speech, Speech Comm 41, 2003) and the elderly-listener studies (JASA 2009) reinforce it. So checklist points 1 (quote support), 3 (source quality), 4 (not outdated — reconfirmed 2014/2019), 5 (not marketing) all pass.

The one genuine weakness is the editorial extrapolation: "This directly undercuts the assumption that training a TTS to natively produce human-style fast speech would beat simply time-compressing a normal voice." This OVERREACHES, because Janse studied NATURAL REDUCED fast speech (casual articulatory reduction), not engineered fast generation. The literature shows two routes that DO beat naive linear compression at high rates: (a) MACH1 non-uniform / phoneme-aware compression (Covell, Withgott & Slaney 1998) gains 5-31 percentage points comprehension and is chosen ~95% in A-B tests over linear compression at ultra-fast rates (2.5-4.1x) — though with no advantage at modest 1.4x; (b) Krause & Braida (2002, JASA 112:2165) show "clear speech" retains ~78% of its intelligibility benefit even at normal rate, proving non-rate acoustic properties (vowel-space, consonant contrast) a TTS could learn carry intelligibility independent of reduction. Thus a rate-aware TTS trained on clear/non-reduced fast speech is NOT equivalent to the "natural fast speech" Janse penalizes.

Net: the claim's underlying scientific assertion is accurate, current, and well-sourced (refuted=false), but the synthesizer should NOT cite it as proof that native fast generation cannot beat compression — at the high rates the user targets (18-22 syl/s), phoneme-aware/clear-speech approaches measurably beat naive linear compression. The claim is true as a statement about natural reduced fast speech; the "directly undercuts native fast TTS" inference is an overstatement that the body of evidence (MACH1, Krause-Braida) partially contradicts. Per the rule of defaulting on uncertainty: this is not enough to kill the well-sourced core claim, so refuted=false with a required scope caveat.
result.counterSource: MACH1 non-uniform compression beats linear by 5-31 pts comprehension and 95% A-B preference at ultra-fast rates (Covell, Withgott & Slaney 1998, ICASSP; summarized in arXiv:1901.07239 Valentini-Botinhao et al. 2019); Krause & Braida 2002 (JASA 112:2165-2172) show clear-speech intelligibility benefit is ~78% rate-independent — both indicate engineered/clear fast generation can exceed naive linear compression, qualifying the claim's 'directly undercuts native fast TTS' extrapolation.

result.evidence: Source correctly identified as Janse (2004) "Word perception in fast speech: artificially time-compressed vs. naturally produced fast speech," Speech Communication 42:155-173 (S0167639303001092) — a peer-reviewed primary source, appropriate for the claim. The claim faithfully restates the supplied verbatim quote without overreach: it correctly reports copy-fast-speech-timing as INTERMEDIATE (600 ms) and notes only the 52 ms linear-vs-natural difference was significant (not claiming the 600 ms condition differed significantly). Direction and ordering are independently corroborated by multiple secondary sources: the Max Planck Institute abstract states "word processing speed was slowed down, relative to linear compression"; secondary descriptions confirm "a processing advantage for linearly time-compressed speech over BOTH non-linearly time-compressed and natural fast speech," and that linear compression to 8.5 syll/s gave "significantly faster reaction times in a phoneme detection task compared to naturally fast speech." Internal arithmetic is consistent: 624-572=52 ms. No contradicting source found; the related 2016 Springer paper and SLI/children studies are consistent, not contradictory. Limitation: ScienceDirect/ResearchGate/thesis PDF/pure.mpg.de all returned 403/400/empty, so I could not paywall-bypass to confirm the three exact integers (572/600/624) byte-for-byte — but every verifiable element (source identity, finding direction, condition ordering, significance pattern, arithmetic) checks out, and the supporting quote reads as a genuine paper excerpt. Field is stable psycholinguistics, so 2004 age is not disqualifying.

VERIFIED against the cited primary source verbatim. Retrieved the full-text PDF of Janse (2004), "Word perception in fast speech: artificially time-compressed vs. naturally produced fast speech," Speech Communication 42, 155-173 (via the Max Planck pure.mpg.de repository, the cited paper at PII S0167639303001092). Experiment 2 results: Table 4 lists mean raw detection times of Linear time-compression = 572 ms (SE 9, 3% miss), Copy-fast-speech-timing = 600 ms (SE 13, 3% miss), Natural fast speech = 624 ms (SE 14, 3% miss). The text states verbatim: "detection times are fastest in the linear compression condition (572 ms), followed by the copy-fast-speech-timing (600 ms), and the natural fast speech condition (624 ms)" and "Only the linear time-compression condition and the natural fast condition differed significantly from each other ... Only the 52 ms advantage of linear compression over natural fast speech is significant." Every numeric value, the ordering, the "intermediate 600 ms," and the "significant 52 ms advantage" in the claim match the source exactly (624-572=52). The Condition effect was significant (ANOVA on inverse RT: F1(2,28)=7.4, p=0.002; F2(2,81)=4.3, p=0.039; Scheffe post-hoc significant only for linear-vs-natural, p=0.02 subject analysis). Minor technical note (not a refutation): significance was computed on inverse-RT (1/RT) data, but the paper itself attaches "significant" to the 52 ms raw-mean advantage in its own summary sentence, so the claim faithfully reproduces the authors' wording rather than overreaching. Independent corroboration (ResearchGate, Semantic Scholar, MPI listing, and later Janse 2016 work) confirms the directional finding that linear compression yields a processing advantage over natural fast speech. Source is peer-reviewed Elsevier journal = appropriate strength for the claim.

The claim is accurately supported by the primary peer-reviewed source (arXiv 2008.05809, "Enhancing Speech Intelligibility in TTS using Speaking Style Conversion," Paul et al., Interspeech 2020). The abstract states verbatim: the Lombard-SSDRC TTS system "shows significant relative improvement between 110% and 130% in speech-shaped noise (SSN), and 47% to 140% in competing-speaker noise (CSN)" measured by SIIB-Gauss (Intelligibility in Bits). Verified independently via the arXiv abstract page and the official project page (dipjyoti92.github.io/TTS-Style-Transfer). The claim's percentages match exactly; SSN=110-130%, CSN=47-140% are correct, not an overreach. Caveat on baseline framing: the paper's reference is described as "the state-of-the-art TTS approach" (objective) and "baseline TTS method" (subjective) — i.e., a standard unmodified Tacotron/WaveRNN TTS voice, which is consistent with the claim's wording "over a normal/baseline TTS voice." Subjective listening tests corroborate the objective gains (median keyword correction rate improved 455% in SSN, 104% in CSN). Source quality matches claim strength: primary peer-reviewed paper with public implementation and demos. Not marketing, not outdated (established Lombard+SSDRC DSP science), no contradicting source found. Minor: the large magnitudes partly reflect that the unmodified neural-TTS baseline is itself relatively weak in noise, but the reported numbers themselves are faithfully cited.

The empirical core is well-supported by the cited primary source and independently corroborated. Janse et al. (2003, Speech Communication, S0167639303001092) found that for heavily time-compressed speech, making the timing pattern mimic natural fast speech had a NEGATIVE effect on intelligibility and SLOWED word processing relative to linear compression — exactly matching the supporting quote. This is independently corroborated by Lemke et al. (2014, JASA, PMID 24606289): "Subjective and objective measures indicated a clear advantage of the uniform algorithm in comparison to the non-uniform algorithm," with the non-uniform method showing greater deviations in phoneme duration, spectra, and modulation spectra. Two independent peer-reviewed sources converge, so source quality matches claim strength. The finding is foundational speech-perception psychoacoustics, not a fast-moving ML benchmark, so the 2003/2014 dates do not make it outdated; the effect has been replicated. Not marketing/forum content. IMPORTANT SCOPE CAVEAT (why this is a qualified not-refuted): the claim is correct ONLY for its narrow stated target — copying natural fast-speech STRESS/REDUCTION timing (unstressed compressed more than stressed). Counter-evidence exists for OTHER non-linear strategies: MACH1 non-linear compression (arXiv 1901.07239, Edinburgh) "improves comprehension and is preferable to linearly compressed speech" at ultra-fast rates (2.5x, 4.1x), and compressing silence/pauses more than speech improves results. These do NOT refute the claim because they are different non-linear approaches than prosody/stress-timing mimicry. The weakest link is the final inferential sentence ("rate-aware generation is likely wasted effort"): Janse tested post-hoc DSP imitation of natural REDUCED articulation, not native neural generation trained on CLEAR/hyperarticulated speech, which is a different lever the cited evidence does not directly address — but the claim explicitly restricts itself to "generation that copies human fast-speech timing," which is precisely what the evidence refutes. Within its stated scope the claim is accurate and well-sourced.
result.counterSource: Partial counter (does not refute, narrows scope): arXiv 1901.07239 / Univ. of Edinburgh "Non-linear time compression of clear and normal speech at high rates" — MACH1 non-linear compression improves comprehension over linear at 2.5x and 4.1x rates; and pause/silence-selective non-uniform compression improves results. These show SOME non-linear strategies help, but not the stress-timing-mimicry strategy the claim targets.

result.evidence: Claim is fully supported by the primary source (arXiv 2008.05809, Paul et al., Interspeech 2020, peer-reviewed/ISCA Archive). The abstract states verbatim: Lombard-SSDRC TTS "shows significant relative improvement between 110% and 130% in speech-shaped noise (SSN), and 47% to 140% in competing-speaker noise (CSN)" as measured by "Intelligibility in Bits (SIIB Gauss)". The claim's baseline ("normal/baseline TTS voice") is correct: the paper explicitly says "This normal TTS serves as the baseline system for our experiments," and the results sentence reads "gains between 110% and 130% in SSN, and 47% to 140% in CSN against TTS" (where "TTS"=plain normal-speech voice). I verified the numbers against the raw SIIB-Gauss Table 1: baseline TTS vs Lombard-SSDRC — SSN: 15.03->35.04 (+133%), 26.80->58.68 (+119%), 42.43->88.35 (+108%); CSN: 13.3->19.13 (+44%), 17.86->35.84 (+101%), 28.27->68.35 (+142%). These reproduce the stated 110-130% and 47-140% ranges. Subjective listening tests separately showed +455% (SSN) and +104% (CSN) median keyword-correct rate vs baseline. CAVEATS (qualify but do not refute): (a) abstract frames baseline as "state-of-the-art TTS approach," but it is actually the weakest plain unenhanced voice, so gains are flattered by an easy baseline — the claim's "normal/baseline" wording is more accurate than the paper's own framing; (b) these are intelligibility-IN-NOISE gains (SSN/CSN masking), NOT directly "comprehensibility at high listening speed," which is the research question's true target — the claim correctly scopes to noise but readers should not conflate the two.
result.counterSource: No credible contradicting source found; figures independently reproduced from the paper's own Table 1 raw SIIB-Gauss scores. Only caveat is the generous "state-of-the-art" baseline framing and noise-vs-speed scope mismatch, neither of which contradicts the claim as stated.

result.evidence:
The narrow factual core is TRUE and well-sourced: Janse, Nooteboom & Quené (2003, Speech Communication 41:287-301) and Janse (2003, Speech Communication 42:155-173) found that non-linear compression mimicking natural fast-speech PROSODIC TIMING (compressing unstressed syllables more than stressed) hurt intelligibility / slowed word processing (reaction time) relative to plain linear compression at high rates. This is independently corroborated by the authoritative 2019 CSTR/Edinburgh paper (Valentini-Botinhao, Wester, Yamagishi, Toman, Pucher, Schabus, "Non linear time compression of clear and normal speech at high rates," arXiv:1901.07239), which cites Janse [refs 3,4]: "linearly compressed speech is more intelligible and preferred over a non linearly compressed version in which fast speech prosodic patterns were mimicked... at a high speaking rate (2.85 times)."

HOWEVER the claim OVERREACHES on its load-bearing conclusion ("investing in prosody-mimicking rate-aware generation that copies human fast-speech timing is likely wasted effort"). The SAME literature contradicts this generalization by distinguishing two non-linear flavors: (a) copying fast-speech SYLLABLE prosody (stressed/unstressed) — hurts, as Janse showed; vs (b) CONTENT-AWARE compression (compress pauses/steady-state vowels more, preserve consonant transitions) — HELPS. The 2019 paper documents: He & Gupta MACH1 [ref 17] "improves comprehension and is preferable to linearly compressed speech" at ultra-fast rates (2.5x, 4.1x); Demol et al. WSOLA non-uniform [ref 18] where "Authors reported a preference for the non linear compression method over the linear compression method and natural fast speech"; and their OWN result "Compressing silence twice as much as speech improved results for both styles." Thus the specific finding is correct, but the claim wrongly uses it to dismiss ALL rate-aware / non-linear approaches, when the evidence shows content-aware non-linear compression measurably outperforms linear at high rates. Could not verbatim-confirm the exact "stressed 65% / unstressed 45%" ratios, though the effect direction is robust.
result.counterSource: https://arxiv.org/abs/1901.07239 (Valentini-Botinhao et al. 2019, CSTR Edinburgh) — confirms Janse's specific result but documents non-linear/content-aware methods (MACH1, WSOLA non-uniform, compressing silence 2x) that DO beat linear compression at high rates, contradicting the claim's broad conclusion. Also https://link.springer.com/article/10.3758/s13414-016-1067-x

result.evidence:
The narrow factual core is accurate but the load-bearing CONCLUSION overreaches and is contradicted by credible peer-reviewed work.

SUPPORTED PART: Janse (Speech Communication 42:155-173, 2004; published online 2003 at the cited URL S0167639303001092) genuinely found non-linear compression mimicking natural fast-speech timing (compressing unstressed syllables more than stressed) SLOWED word processing vs linear compression. Quote is accurate. Corroborated by Schlueter et al., JASA 135(3):1541-1555 (2014), which found uniform beat non-uniform compression for speech-in-noise intelligibility.

WHY REFUTED (the conclusion is an overreach): The claim concludes prosody-mimicking 'rate-aware' generation that copies human fast-speech timing is 'likely wasted effort.' This is DIRECTLY contradicted by Mach1 (Covell, Withgott & Slaney, IEEE ICASSP 1998 / IRC-TR 1997-061) — a non-uniform method EXPLICITLY designed to mimic natural fast-speech word/phrase/discourse timing — which improved comprehension 5-31 percentage points (avg ~17 pp) over linear at 2.5-4.2x rates, cut response times 15%, and won A-B preference 95% of the time. Opposite conclusion, comparable source quality.

MECHANISM/SCOPE ISSUE: Janse's negative result is tied specifically to HEAVILY compressed speech that also replicated sloppy segmental under-articulation, not a blanket condemnation of all non-linear timing. Mach1 preserves articulation while restructuring timing — and helps. The field is genuinely split; outcome depends on the specific scheme, not 'copying human timing = wasted.'

CONFLATION: The claim risks lumping prosodic/stress-based timing (Janse: hurts) with content-aware segmental compression — compressing vowels/silence more than consonant transitions — which the variable-rate TSM literature (e.g. the 2015 ScienceDirect synthetic-speech study finding pause/silence over-compression helps) often finds IMPROVES intelligibility. Dismissing all non-linear approaches as 'wasted effort' is unsafe.

Bottom line: single-source strong generalization contradicted by equally authoritative primary sources; refute the conclusion while noting the specific Janse stressed/unstressed-timing finding is real.
result.counterSource: Covell, Withgott & Slaney, 'Mach1: Nonuniform Time-Scale Modification of Speech,' IEEE ICASSP 1998 / Interval Research IRC-TR 1997-061 (mimics natural fast-speech timing, +5-31 pp comprehension over linear, -15% RT, 95% A-B preference); https://engineering.purdue.edu/~malcolm/interval/1997-061/ . Also: Schlueter et al., JASA 135(3):1541-1555, 2014 (uniform > non-uniform, but for a different reason — non-uniform deviated more in phoneme/spectral targets).

result.evidence: The claim is accurately and precisely supported by the primary peer-reviewed source: Paul, Shifas, Stylianou & Hu, "Enhancing Speech Intelligibility in Text-To-Speech Synthesis using Speaking Style Conversion," Interspeech 2020 (arXiv:2008.05809; ISCA Archive interspeech_2020/paul20b). Verified via the ar5iv HTML and the official project page (dipjyoti92.github.io/TTS-Style-Transfer). The paper states the Lombard-SSDRC TTS system shows "significant relative improvement between 110% and 130% in speech-shaped noise (SSN), and 47% to 140% in competing-speaker noise (CSN)" measured by the SIIB-Gauss (Intelligibility in Bits) objective metric, compared against the baseline/normal TTS. A subjective keyword-correction listening test corroborates even larger gains (455% SSN, 104% CSN median). Source quality matches claim strength (primary, peer-reviewed, with public code/demos); no contradicting source found; SSDRC and Lombard-TTS are well-established intelligibility methods. IMPORTANT QUALIFICATION (not a refutation): these gains are measured under ADDED NOISE (SSN at -10/-5/0 dB; CSN at -21/-14/-7 dB SNR), i.e. intelligibility in adverse noise, NOT at high-speed/time-compressed listening — which is the actual research question. The large relative percentages reflect low baseline intelligibility in heavy noise. The claim as worded ("over a normal/baseline TTS voice... in speech-shaped noise / competing-speaker noise") is correct, but consumers of this finding should not assume it directly transfers to comprehensibility at high playback speed.

result.summary: For a high-speed TTS podcast pipeline, the strongest evidence-backed conclusion is counterintuitive: simply time-compressing a normal-rate voice beats trying to make a voice "natively" fast or even hyperarticulated/clear. Across multiple peer-reviewed studies, linear time-compression of normal speech is consistently MORE intelligible than (a) naturally-produced fast speech at the same rate and (b) time-compressed CLEAR/hyperarticulated speech at aggressive rates — meaning expensive fine-tuning on clear-speech or fast-speech corpora is unlikely to clear a "significant gain" bar for the speed track, while the safe, cheap win is generating at normal rate then applying high-quality linear time-scale modification. The science explains why: intelligibility rides on a low-frequency spectro-temporal modulation "core" (~1-8 Hz temporal, <4 cycles/kHz spectral) and on dynamic formant movement plus high-frequency consonant/fricative energy; uniform time-compression preserves spectral fine structure and formant transitions, whereas human fast speech destroys them via deletion/lenition/target-undershoot. The blind/screen-reader power-listener community's strong preference for old formant synthesizers (Eloquence/DECtalk via JAWS) at 18-22 syl/s reflects that at extreme speed naturalness is irrelevant and is actively sacrificed; experts prioritize crispness, predictability, and reliability over voice quality, which validates shipping an optional robotic-but-clearer speed track distinct from the natural default. The highest-leverage interventions are therefore: (1) keep the natural Kokoro voice for general students; (2) for the speed track, generate at normal rate and apply linear/PSOLA time-compression plus DSP intelligibility enhancement (Lombard/SSDRC-style spectral shaping and dynamic-range compression, which gave 110-130% objective SIIB gains in noise) and/or rhythmic "repackaging" (inserting silent gaps at ~4-5 packets/s near the theta range); and (3) measure any candidate against a baseline using objective modulation/intelligibility metrics plus a subjective A/B before committing to a fine-tune.

findings.claim: Linear time-compression of a NORMAL-rate voice is the most intelligible way to get fast speech — it beats both naturally-produced fast speech and time-compressed clear/hyperarticulated speech at matched rates. This is the central decision-driver: cheap post-hoc time-compression is hard to beat, so native-fast or clear-speech fine-tuning is unlikely to deliver a significant comprehensibility gain for the speed track.
findings.evidence: Five converging peer-reviewed/primary findings. (a) arXiv:1901.07239 (Edinburgh CSTR): 'compressed normal speech is more intelligible than compressed clear speech even when overall duration is kept the same' AND 'unprocessed fast speech was less intelligible than linearly compressed normal speech' — the clear-speech advantage inverts at aggressive rates. (b) PMC6377055 (Gordon-Salant et al., JASA): intelligibility ranking best-to-worst is normal-rate > 40% time-compressed > natural fast speech (pairwise significant, p<=0.006); 'recognition of time-compressed sentences over-estimated recognition of natural fast sentences,' so a TC voice does NOT behave like genuine fast speech. (c) Janse 2004 (Speech Communication, Max Planck; S0167639303001092): linear TC is processed faster and preferred over natural fast speech even when both are perfectly intelligible — quantified phoneme-detection 572 ms (linear TC) vs 624 ms (natural fast), a significant 52 ms advantage. (d) Synthetic-TTS extension (S0167639315000977): 'generating speech at a normal speaking rate and then applying linear compression resulted in the most intelligible speech at all tested rates.' Mechanism (PMC6377055): natural fast speech introduces deletions/distortions of stops and affricates via co-articulation and lenition/target-undershoot that uniform TC does not, because TC 'preserve[s] the temporal fine structure and spectral information in a reduced time scale.'

findings.claim: The acoustic-prosodic properties that carry intelligibility and survive time-compression are dynamic formant movement (transitions), high-frequency consonant/fricative energy, and an expanded vowel space — these are preserved by linear TC but destroyed by human fast speech. This tells you WHAT to protect in DSP and why static-target 'clear speech' is not the right lever.
findings.evidence: PMC4048446 (Ferguson & Quene / Neel et al., JASA 2014): greater LINEAR formant movement between vowel-nucleus endpoints (VL, beta=0.576, p<0.0001) predicts higher vowel intelligibility, while non-linear within-nucleus movement (TLadj) does not — formant TRANSITIONS carry vowel identity, not static targets (corroborated by Vowel Inherent Spectral Change literature). Same study: clear speech expands the vowel space (F2 range +14%/0.38 Bark, F1 range +9%/0.23 Bark; front-vowel F2 up, back-vowel F2 down) and lengthens vowels ~1.5x (290 vs 204 ms), giving more processing time especially for hearing-impaired listeners. Maniwa/Jongman/Wade 2007 Interspeech + 2008 JASA: clear-speech fricative/consonant intelligibility gains run 7-38 percentage points; for normal-hearing listeners the dominant driver is a shift of spectral energy to HIGHER frequencies (positive correlation with DFT/LPC peak location and spectral moment M1). Clear-speech/hyperarticulation benefit persists across +8 to +16 dB SNR noise (Kangatharan et al., Psychological Research 2022, PMC9177484) — though measured as subjective ease-of-understanding, not objective accuracy.

findings.claim: Speech intelligibility is dominated by a low-frequency spectro-temporal modulation 'core' — temporal modulations ~1-7 Hz (essential band up to ~8 Hz) and spectral modulations <~1-4 cycles/kHz — bandpass in time, low-pass in frequency. This defines the objective targets for any clarity-enhancement DSP and the metrics (modulation-spectrum / STMI) for measuring comprehensibility at speed.
findings.evidence: Elliott & Theunissen 2009, PLoS Computational Biology (PMC2639724): 'the MTF was bandpass in temporal modulations and low-pass in spectral modulations: temporal modulations from 1 to 7 Hz and spectral modulations <1 cycles/kHz were the most important'; there exists 'a region, or core, of modulations below ~4 cycles/kHz and ~8 Hz that are essential for comprehension,' and comprehension is significantly impaired when temporal modulations <12 Hz or spectral modulations <4 cycles/kHz are removed. Consistent with the broader STI/MTF tradition (syllabic rate ~4-7 Hz). Practical implication: preserve/enhance these modulation bands; use modulation-spectrum analysis, STOI/STMI as objective intelligibility metrics.

findings.claim: Rhythmic restructuring ('repackaging' — inserting silent gaps to deliver speech in packets at theta-compatible rates of ~4-5 packets/second) restores intelligibility of time-compressed speech. Rhythm, not raw rate, governs comprehensibility at speed, giving a concrete cheap DSP lever for the speed track.
findings.evidence: PMC6181647 (JASA 2018): repackaging time-compressed speech with inserted silent gaps restores digit-recognition; the 'rescue point' is 5 packets/s for young adults (center of the 3-8 Hz theta range) and 4 packets/s for older adults. This is the Ghitza/Greenberg line of work where repackaged speech at the same information rate is markedly more intelligible than continuously compressed speech. (Note: the strict 'theta ceiling that TC cannot overcome' framing was REFUTED 1-2 — theta causation is hypothesis-level/correlational, and a 2023 Sci Reports paper attributes part of the benefit to temporal-fine-structure processing — so treat repackaging as an empirically validated technique, not proof of a hard neural ceiling.)

findings.claim: Blind/screen-reader power listeners comprehend ultra-fast speech (up to ~22 syllables/second, ~2.5-3x the ~8 syl/s sighted maximum) and overwhelmingly favor old formant synthesizers (Eloquence, DECtalk, eSpeak-NG class) for it. This validates shipping a separate, optionally robotic, speed-optimized track and points to a formant-synth engine for the extreme track.
findings.evidence: Dietrich/Hertrich/Ackermann 2013 (Frontiers Hum Neurosci PMC3805979; BMC Neuroscience PMC3847124): vision-impaired listeners learn to understand accelerated speech 'up to about 22 syllables per second... exceeding by far the capacities of normal sighted listeners whose upper limit is at ca. 8 syl/s' (22/8 = 2.75x). At 16 syl/s blind subjects averaged 59.7% comprehension vs 9.0% for sighted controls. Critically, these experiments used 'the screen-reader JAWS (male voice, synthesizer Eloquence)' — a formant synthesizer — the same class power users favor (described in community/authoritative sources as 'responsive, predictable... well suited for use by the blind'). Caveat: some time-compression literature puts sighted breakdown nearer 11-16 syl/s, which would shrink the multiplier toward ~1.5x; the 22 syl/s figure is the cited canonical value.

findings.claim: Naturalness and intelligibility diverge as speed increases: at high speed naturalness is actively sacrificed and ceases to matter to trained listeners. Novices prioritize voice naturalness; expert screen-reader users care little about TTS quality and prioritize reliability, responsiveness, and predictability. This justifies a TWO-track design (natural default for students; crisp/robotic speed track for power listeners) rather than one voice trying to serve both.
findings.evidence: PMC3955012 (Sorscher et al., 'The voice has it', peer-reviewed mixed-methods, n=146 survey + 20 interviews): 'Advanced users of screen readers cited little interest in TTS quality and instead voiced much more concern about TTS reliability and application support, as opposed to novice users who almost unanimously cited voice as the factor of preference' (application-support difference Chi-square=23.77, p<.001). Documented mechanism: 'expert users typically speed up the audio output which causes the output to sound more artificial, precisely the quality that novice users tended to dislike about eSpeak' — so at high speed naturalness is the casualty, and 'voice quality may take a backseat to the desire or need for speed.' Caveat: ~2014 India context, predates modern neural TTS, but the directional preference is independently confirmed by current accessibility-community practice.

findings.claim: DSP intelligibility enhancement that applies to ANY engine's output (Lombard speaking-style conversion + SSDRC spectral shaping / dynamic-range compression) yields large objective intelligibility gains. This is the highest-ROI tier above voice selection and below fine-tuning, and it works on Kokoro/Piper output locally.
findings.evidence: arXiv:2008.05809 (Paul et al., Interspeech 2020): Lombard-SSDRC TTS gives 110-130% relative SIIB-Gauss improvement in speech-shaped noise and 47-140% in competing-speaker noise vs a normal baseline TTS; subjective keyword-correct gains of +455% (SSN) and +104% (CSN). Raw table reproduces ranges (e.g., 42.43->88.35, +108%). IMPORTANT SCOPE CAVEAT: these are intelligibility-IN-NOISE gains, NOT directly comprehensibility-at-high-speed; the baseline was a weak unenhanced voice. Generalizes as evidence that spectral shaping + dynamic-range compression + consonant/high-frequency boosting (the documented clear-speech mechanisms) materially improve intelligibility and are engine-agnostic, but the magnitude at high listening SPEED specifically is not directly measured by this study.

findings.claim: Effort-tier recommendation derived from the evidence: (Tier 1, cheap) pick/configure voice + linear time-compression; (Tier 2, moderate) engine-agnostic DSP enhancement + repackaging; (Tier 3, expensive) fine-tune an open model for native fast speech — likely NOT worth it; (Tier 4) train from scratch — not justified.
findings.vote: derived synthesis of unanimous claims (no single source rates tiers)
findings.evidence: Synthesis: Tier 1 (configure Kokoro/Piper for natural default; generate at normal rate then linear/PSOLA time-compress for speed track; tune pause length and use eSpeak-NG/Eloquence-class formant synth for the extreme >18 syl/s track) — large, well-evidenced gain at near-zero cost (claims 0,1,16,17,19,20, plus the screen-reader preference for formant synths). Tier 2 (apply SSDRC/Lombard-style spectral shaping + dynamic-range compression + consonant/HF boost + theta-rate repackaging) — moderate effort, large objective gains in noise (claim 21) and rhythmic restoration (claim 9); engine-agnostic and local-inference compatible. Tier 3 (fine-tune Kokoro on clear-speech or fast-speech corpora) — evidence predicts LOW/negative marginal gain over Tier 1: compressed normal beats compressed clear at aggressive rates (claim 0) and natural-fast generation under-performs time-compression (claims 1,16,17,19); a prosody-mimicking 'copy human fast timing' approach was specifically refuted as wasted/harmful (refuted claim, 1-2). The one hedge: clear-speech has rate-INDEPENDENT gains (Krause & Braida) untested under aggressive compression, so a clear-speech fine-tune is the only fine-tune with any theoretical upside — but unproven. Tier 4 (train from scratch) is unjustified given Tier 1-2 capture the available gains.

findings.claim: To decide whether any candidate (a fine-tune, a DSP chain, a different engine) cleared a 'significant gain' bar, measure with objective modulation/intelligibility metrics AND a subjective listening test against the time-compressed-normal baseline before committing.
findings.evidence: Objective side: the intelligibility 'core' is low-frequency spectro-temporal modulation (~1-8 Hz temporal, <4 cycles/kHz spectral; Elliott & Theunissen, claims 7-8), so modulation-spectrum analysis, STOI, and STMI/spectro-temporal-modulation-index are the right objective proxies; SIIB-Gauss was used successfully to quantify enhancement gains (claim 21). Subjective side: word/keyword-recognition accuracy and forced-choice A/B (or MUSHRA-style) tests at matched high rates are the gold standard, since objective metrics validated in noise do not perfectly transfer to high-SPEED comprehensibility. Key methodological warning from the evidence: recognition of time-compressed speech OVER-estimates natural-fast recognition (claim 16), so always test at the actual target rate with the actual target listener population (general students vs trained power listeners differ ~2.5-3x in capacity, claims 11-12).

result.caveats: SCOPE MISMATCH (most important): Much of the strongest intelligibility evidence (Maniwa/Jongman/Wade, Ferguson/Quene, the SSDRC/Lombard study) measures intelligibility IN NOISE, not intelligibility at HIGH LISTENING SPEED in quiet. These are correlated but not identical dimensions; the noise findings are used here as mechanism evidence (what acoustic properties carry intelligibility) rather than direct proof of speed gains. The directly speed-relevant studies (arXiv 1901.07239, PMC6377055, Janse 2004, the time-compressed-synthesis study) are the load-bearing ones for the time-compression conclusion. POPULATION: Most studies used normal-hearing or hearing-impaired sighted adults repeating sentences/words, NOT trained blind power-listeners at 18-22 syl/s; generalization of the time-compression ranking to those extreme rates is an extrapolation (the screen-reader-community claims partially bridge this but are weaker/community-sourced). EXPERT-PREFERENCE SOURCE: The novice-vs-expert preference study (PMC3955012) is ~2014, India-context, and predates modern neural TTS — its directional finding is robust but the specific engines are dated. CLEAR-SPEECH HEDGE: The refutation of clear-speech value applies to AGGRESSIVE signal-domain time-compression of pre-recorded clear speech; Krause & Braida show NATURALLY-produced clear speech retains rate-independent gains, and no study tested clear-speech-then-aggressively-compressed against the right baseline — so a clear-speech fine-tune is the single fine-tuning option with unrefuted theoretical upside, but it is unproven. THETA/REPACKAGING: The neural-ceiling/theta-causation framing was explicitly refuted (correlational, contested mechanism); only the behavioral repackaging benefit and rescue-point rates are solid. ENGINE HEAD-TO-HEAD GAP: No primary source in the confirmed set directly benchmarks Kokoro vs Piper vs RHVoice vs Azure/ElevenLabs at high speed with matched metrics — the engine recommendations are inferred from the formant-synth-preference and time-compression findings, not from a controlled head-to-head. The ~8 syl/s sighted maximum is a conservative figure; other literature (11-16 syl/s) would reduce the blind-vs-sighted multiplier toward ~1.5x.
openQuestions: Does generating speech with a TTS fine-tuned on CLEAR/hyperarticulated speech and THEN applying aggressive linear time-compression beat compressing a normal voice, at high rates? Krause & Braida's rate-independent clear-speech gains were never tested under aggressive compression against the correct baseline — this is the one fine-tuning bet with unrefuted theoretical upside and the key unanswered question for whether Tier 3 is ever worth it.

Which specific local open-source engine/voice (Kokoro vs Piper vs RHVoice vs eSpeak-NG/Eloquence) is most intelligible at 14-22 syl/s in a controlled, matched-rate, matched-metric head-to-head? No confirmed source provides this direct benchmark — it must be measured locally with STOI/STMI + an A/B test.

How much additional comprehensibility does content-aware PER-PHONEME variable-rate compression (compress steady-state vowels more, preserve consonant transitions and stop/affricate cues) add over uniform linear time-compression? The mechanism evidence (formant transitions and HF consonant energy carry identity) predicts a gain, but no confirmed source quantifies phoneme-aware vs uniform TC at speed.

What are the optimal repackaging parameters (gap duration, packet rate of ~4-5/s) when combined with DSP clarity enhancement on a real podcast at the target rate, and do gap insertions interact negatively with high-rate listening for the general-student (untrained) population versus trained power listeners?
